experiments.title: How personalization and brand reduction affect conversion on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: How will personalization and brand reduction affect conversion on a post-survey donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2020 - 10/17/2020
Hillsdale College had run an experiment during the pre-launch of their Introduction to Western Philosophy course that tested two variables independently. First, they tested using personalization in the headline of the “instant” donation page to increase the buy-in of the registrant and get them reading the page. Secondly, they noticed that the donation page had many mentions of Hillsdale College, which seemed redundant and seemed to get the brand “in the way” of the relationship that the copy was attempting to develop with the donor. They tested a separate treatment that did NOT include the personalization but did reduce the mentions of the brand, substituting pronouns where appropriate. These two treatments both appeared to lift revenue, 19% and 13% respectively. They wanted to re-test this, and combine the elements into one treatment. They chose their Electoral College Survey, developed the treatment, and launched a test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which combined the personalization and the reduced brand mention, increased revenue by 13.4%. This reinforces the learning from the initial experiment, and shows that a similar lift in revenue holds for a treatment on a different offer, especially when combining the treatments.
This experiment results suggests that:
Personalization has a statistically significant impact on conversion. Reducing the mentions of the brand on the donation page has a statistically significant impact on conversion. Both elements combined contribute to a lift in average gift and revenue per visitor.

experiments.title: How increasing the size of a sticky bar (while asking for more information) affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing the size of a sticky bar (while asking for more information) affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:

experiments.title: How messaging based on donor lifecycle affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will segmenting with specific messages based on donor lifecycle increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2018
Hillsdale College had recently integrated donor lifecycle messaging into their email platform and wanted to test the impact of using this segmentation (with appropriate messaging) in their year-end campaign. They had an appeal ready to go that asked donors and prospects to give a gift to send Pocket Constitutions to public elementary schools. In the CTA of this email (which existed in the final paragraph), they inserted segment-based language that was specific to nondonors, lapsed donors, recurring donors, and active donors. The control group got the same ask, regardless of lifecycle stage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In aggregate, the treatments with lifecycle messaging lifted donor conversion by 29.1%, resulting in a 34% increase to revenue. However, each individual segment performed differently. The results with the non-donor segment (where the message indicated that Hillsdale knew they had never made a gift before) increased conversion by 58.4%—by far the biggest increase.
There was no difference in the recurring segment, which was a relatively small group of people. The lapsed segment saw a 6% increase (though it wasn't valid due to sample size), and the donor segment saw a slight 4% lift, which was also not statistically valid.
So, to answer the question—should we spend the time to segment and tailor our messaging to our donors based on their donor status? The answer is a resounding yes, especially for nondonors. If you only have time to tailor one segment, write messaging for nondonors and donors. But, if you're able to write more copy, you may see gains across the board.

experiments.title: How video affects conversion on a campaign donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: How will video affect conversion on a campaign donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2020 - 06/28/2020
Hillsdale College was running their fiscal year end campaign. Their team had generated a video designed to inspire giving, which they planned to use in cultivation leading up to the campaign. However, they wanted to test to see if it would improve conversion on the main donation page. Historically, they had seen that video decreased conversion on donation pages, but given the increase of their audience and the passage of time, they wanted to see if that still held true. They created an alternate donation page that included the video but held all other variables the same and split the traffic to determine the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the video reduced conversion by 20.9% in aggregate. Further segment analysis revealed that mobile traffic was the most affected, where the video reduced conversion by 37.5%.
There are many hypotheses as to why this happens. Often, the video might be a diversion from the real intent, which is giving. Or, on mobile, the video might overtake the screen entirely and remove the donation form and copy from view.
This shows that video, while there might be benefits in other ways to assist giving, does decrease conversion when added directly to the donation page.

experiments.title: How a defense-minded approach with an expanded value proposition affects conversion rate

They hypothesized that a stronger posture that explained the “why” behind the importance of education—defending liberty. The copy also did more to explain the problem to which Hillsdale’s educational efforts provides a solution, and gave extra descriptive copy of the premium. experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the extended value proposition and defense-minded headline, decreased conversion rate by 11.5%. This showed that a positive message in this case (plus the benefit of shorter copy) was more likely to inspire a gift.
This could reflect the mindset of donors while they are on the course platform—they are not thinking about defense, but opportunity. Further testing might expand opportunity there.

However, the size of the sticky bar that held these fields was not held constant.  The treatment had a much larger footprint, which led to some part of the increase. experiments.keyLearnings: The new experiment showed that the additional form fields did contribute to the lift, but not the full 83.5% lift. The additional form fields produced a 28.7% lift in conversion, which is fascinating as it goes against conventional wisdom that more fields equals more friction. It's important to note that Hillsdale generates more value from the additional fields, as it gets to address users downstream by their first and last name, which has proven effects on long-term value.

experiments.title: How a problem solution framework affects email clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a problem solution framework affect email clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/14/2020
Hillsdale College launched a new course called introduction to Western philosophy. They had written and email prospecting treatment that describe to the course, and told the reader that they believed that philosophy was for everyone. The treatment email gave a preview of the course content and invited them to pre-register. However they wondered if framing the necessity for the course by describing the problem that our country finds itself in would increase the desire of the reader to take the course. The draft of the treatment that talked about how people have forgotten to reason and deliberate, instead choosing just to react. Then, the email positioned the course as a solution to this problem, as it would help build a defense against dangerous ideologies and prevent their spread. They launched an A/B test with three different email list brokers to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the problem solution framework, produced a 38.3% increase in click through rate. This Illustrated the principle that simply describing a course offering, or any sort of offer, is not enough. Copy must motivate the reader to want to take action, even if they do not perceive themselves to be in the group affected by the problem.
Understanding the reason a course was created, in this case, helped incentivize people to sign up for it—even though they knew less about what was in the course. This shows that emotion can overpower simple facts.

experiments.title: How removing a step from a signup process affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will removal of a step from a signup process affects conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/22/2019
Hillsdale College had previously tested into a two-step course signup process that increased donations by increasing signups, and thus traffic to the donation page. However, several years later, they no longer needed home address information upfront, as it would be gathered downstream. They decided to test the impact of removing the second step from their course signup process to see how sending people directly to the donation page (the final step in the process) affected donation revenue. The hypothesis was that more traffic to the donation page (by using the single-step process) would simplify the registration process and deliver more donor prospects.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Surprisingly, removing the second step decreased conversion by 18.5%. While 6% more people got to the page (as the second step had a 94% conversion rate), those people who got there through a single step gave much less often. There are a few potential reasons for this:
The second step increased the required investment and made them more likely to continue with the third form.The second step increased the perceived value of the course and made it more appealing to give.Donors who fill out two preceding forms are more likely to fill out the third.
Either way, this experiment shuttered Hillsdale's plans to remove second steps from all course signup flows.

experiments.title: The impact of direct mail cultivation on donor retention
experiments.researchQuestion: Does sending cultivation and stewardship-focused post cards increase donor retention?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2018 - 06/10/2019
Hillsdale College was looking for new ways to help retain existing donors. Knowing the importance of good cultivation and stewardship, we wanted to focus on methods that could improve the frequency and method for communicating impact and gratitude to the donors. One hypothesis was to send post cards throughout the year that both thanked them for their support and reminded them of the impact their gift.
To do this, we isolated 350K donors and split it randomly in such a way that there would be equal representation for each donor lifecycle segment. Half of these donors would receive four post cards throughout the year in addition to the normal communication stream. The control group would just get the standard communications.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Given the large sample size, we were able to validate a 1.8% increase in donor retention for those donors that received the post cards. But this is only part of the story...
When we exclude major gifts, we discovered there was no statistically significant change in revenue per donor. In fact, overall revenue was close to equal between the two segments. While we lifted retention by 1.8%, we saw a similar decrease in revenue per donor (not statistically significant) which resulted in the near-equal total revenue.
When we dug into the individual lifecycle segments, we did see some interesting trends:
2nd Year Donors - No statistically significant change in retention2nd Year Reactivated - No statistically significant change in retentionKey Multiyear - 1.7% increase in retention13-24 Mo. Lapsed Reactivated - 2.3% increase reactivation with a 91% level of confidence25+ Mo. Lapsed Reactivated - 5.6% increase in reactivation
In summary, we saw statistically significant lifts when reactivating lapsed donors and retaining long-time donors. It did not have much of an impact for the donors that had been acquired (or reacquired) the previous year.
When we factor in the costs to send the post cards and compare it to the incremental donors that were retained / reactivated, the total cost came to $157 cost to retain / reactivate. (This is the total mailing costs divided by the 1,099 incremental donors.)
Given these incremental costs with only a minor lift to retention (and no impact on revenue), this style of cultivation does not seem to be sustainable long term. It would be worth retesting the concept if we can reduce the number of mailings and / or potentially increasing the targeting and relevancy based upon the donor's anniversary or specific appeal mailings throughout the year.

experiments.title: How multichannel cultivation impacts year end giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will sending a personalized cultivation postcard via direct mail have an impact on the donors’ likelihood to give and amount given at calendar year end?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2016 - 12/31/2016
Multichannel communication is a known “best practice”, but traditionally refers to the tactic of trying to develop online cultivation with offline donors to increase offline giving. Hillsdale College wondered if the strategy could be reversed—using direct mail to develop offline cultivation with online donors to increase online giving. There were some obvious risks to such a test—particularly the upfront cost of direct mail cultivation.
Leading into the calendar year end campaign, Hillsdale had discovered a technology that would allow them to send personalized post cards to their donors at a reasonably efficient price point. The cards included the donor’s name on the front and a link to a custom video; all of which would be trackable back to the individual donor. The landing page for the video had a donation form with a “soft ask”, but the true test was to see whether direct mail cultivation would increase giving for a digital campaign.
We decided to test out the impact of this kind of technology by sending a postcard to a random segment of active donors for the sole purpose of cultivation and stewardship. We sent this just before Thanksgiving and then monitored their giving throughout the calendar year-end campaign. We would then compare their giving to a similar group of donors to get an understanding of the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results from this test were very encouraging. The donors that received the postcard cultivation saw a statistically significant 204% increase in their year end giving. Additionally, Hillsdale saw a directional 105% increase in their average gift when compared to the control group (although this average gift increase did not reach statistical significance.)

This cultivation strategy resulted in a significant increase in the amount of revenue received from this test group. As organizations become more intentional about growing online donor files, direct mail has the possibility to become a great cultivation channel—with the caveat that online donors might not become offline donors. But if the direct mail pieces can be cost effective, it can help bolster online fundraising results. This is the first of many tests in this area, but one with promising results.

experiments.title: How targeting direct mail prospects with digital advertising affected direct mail revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing ads to a targeted group of direct mail recipients result in increased revenue from the treatment group?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2017 - 10/02/2017
Hillsdale College had significant investments in their direct mail program and wanted to see if they could optimize the return by investing in digital marketing that would target these prospects in the time period that they received a direct mail letter.
They created a control audience that was excluded from seeing any ads, and a treatment audience that would see a rotation of brand and course offering ads (but no donation-centric ads) for a two week period before the mail piece hit mailboxes and for two weeks after, the prime time in which a donor would respond. They optimized the ads for reach—aiming to show ads to as large a percentage of the target group as possible. The goal of this test was to lift direct mail revenue—not to add an additional segment of online revenue.
They spent just shy of $1,000 to show ads to the treatment audience and waited to see the results come in.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Just increasing the number of brand impressions during that time period to a targeted audience resulted in a 145.5% increase in revenue from the treatment group. This showed an incredible value in multichannel targeting—even if the digital ads weren’t focused on direct response.

While this test is quite promising (especially for an August appeal), more testing must be done to ensure that it holds through all seasons and a variety of direct mail offers.

experiments.title: How ad copy call-to-action affects clickthrough rate

Since the audience had already received an email, they created ads with a very direct call-to-action: Sign Up and Reserve Your Seat Now. This assumed that their audience had read the email and knew what to expect, and only needed to be prompted to sign up.
However, they developed a hypothesis that a large portion of the audience might not have engaged with the email, and needed a softer call-to-action. They made a second ad with the same image and copy, but with a headline and button copy that used “Learn More” as the call-to-action. Then, they ran an A/B test to determine which ad attracted more clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment ads produced a 501.7% lift in clickthrough rate, which told Hillsdale that their target audience needed a lot more information before making a decision on whether to attend. They also analyzed more data to determine whether the softer call-to-action had a similar effect on conversion rate.

experiments.title: How aligning with visitor motivation affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: What ad more closely aligns with visitor motivations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2015 - 05/26/2015
Hillsdale College found Facebook to be an effective channel to promote their popular Constitution 101 course. They wanted to test a variety of ads to see which ones attracted more clicks and resulted in more course signups. They created two different ads, each appealing to different motivations. The control offered viewers the opportunity to “Become a Constitution Expert” and invited them to “get” their free course, using a CTA that had proved to be powerful in past tests. This ad also used Facebook’s CTA button to ask viewers to “sign up”. The treatment offered viewers the chance to “Understand the Constitution like never before”, with a CTA to “activate” their free course now. This ad used Facebook’s CTA button to ask users to “learn more”, a softer ask than the control. The ads were launched on Facebook with a split test to evenly distribute results.

experiments.title: How ad copy can affect visitor motivation in a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the length and depth of the value proposition through the ad copy attract more people to sign up for the course?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2017 - 02/08/2017
Hillsdale College launched new creative on Facebook promoting their course on the life of C.S. Lewis. Since these ads are direct response ads designed to attract viewers to sign up for the course, they had previously experienced much success with concise, clear copy that asked people to sign up.
However, they felt that they might have saturated the market of people who needed minimal persuasion to sign up, and hypothesized that to engage the next, less-motivated layer of the target audience, their ads might actually need more copy. They crafted copy for the top section of the ad that gave more insight into who C.S. Lewis was, and made a softer offer to sign up for the course.
This new copy produced a 421.6% increase in clickthrough rate, but they wanted to test further along in the funnel to see if these visitors actually converted more often than the more direct ad copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new copy produced a 409.7% increase in email acquisition rate, which proved that the more engaging copy (combined with a “Find out more” call-to-action led more people into the funnel and also produced more motivated visitors. These results were reflective of two things:
The increased ad copy created a more engaging ad that attracted more clicks and turned those clicks into subscribers.The audience had changed from a group of people who needed very little value proposition to sign up (they were “pre-converted”) to people who needed more value proposition to incentivize them to start the course.
Because this audience had shifted over 18 months of running ads for this course, they realized that they needed to adjust creative to fit their new audience. This process of perpetual optimization is a reminder that the target audience isn’t static—it shifts and moves as you work your way from the center of the target outwards.

experiments.title: How using a timer to reduce anxiety affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the addition of a countdown timer give visitors more reasons to stay on the donation page which will then lead to a higher conversion rate after they consume the content?

As part of the ongoing efforts to optimize the contextualized donation page for their most popular online course, Constitution 101, Hillsdale College created a new treatment to two goals in mind. The idea was to create a reason for visitors to stay on the donation page for a longer time period and to help mitigate complaints that arose when people felt the courses were not actually free.
To accomplish these two goals, we created a new treatment of the donation page that contained a countdown timer letting the visitor know that their course was being prepared and would be ready in a minute and a half. experiments.keyLearnings: The countdown timer decreased conversion rate by 26.9% for visitors on the page. Deeper analysis found that peopler were staying on the page for a longer period of time but this did not lead to increased conversions.  Our hypothesis is that by giving them the exact time until the course started, they could open a new tab in their browser, effectively ignoring the donation page, and come back to the page after a minute and a half to begin the process.

experiments.title: How multichannel cultivation impacts year end giving
experiments.researchQuestion: “Will sending a personalized cultivation message via mail have an impact on the donors’ likelihood to give and amount given at calendar year end?”
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/18/2017
See http://www.nextafter.com/research/2017/05/how-multichannel-cultivation-impacted-year-end-giving/ “The impact of cultivation and stewardship is something that is often hard to measure. We know that we need to thank our donors but it is difficult to put the exact impact this kind of communication has on their likelihood to give. Leading into the calendar year end campaign, Hillsdale had discovered a technology that would allow them to send personalized post cards to their donors. The cards would include their name on the front and would contain a link to a custom video; all of which would be trackable back to the individual donor. We decided to test out the impact of this kind of technology by sending a post card to a random segment of active donors for the sole purpose of cultivation and stewardship. We sent this at Thanksgiving time and then monitored their giving throughout the coming year end campaign. We were able to then compare their giving to a similar group of donors to get an understanding of the impact.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
"The donors that received the post card cultivation saw a statistically significant 204% increase in their year end giving. Additionally, we saw a 105% increase in their average gift when compared to the control group (although this average gift increase did not reach statistical significance.)
This increase in giving resulted in a significant increase in the amount of revenue received from this test group. It reinforces the need for good stewardship and cultivation, especially when leading in to the major giving times of the year."

Note: 293,000 was the total number of samples. The numbers of conversions were included to reach correct conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding a premium book offer affects donor revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will adding a book as a premium offer affect conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2017 - 02/13/2017
Timeframe: 2/1/2017 – 2/13/2017
Hillsdale College was promoting their new online course, The U.S. Supreme Court. They were acquiring course subscribers and email addresses at a decent rate, but donor conversion was well below average, which led to a much higher cost-per-donor. They wanted to test whether adding a premium offer would increase donor conversion, by giving new donors something tangible to go along with the course material. They also hypothesized that their premium book, The U.S. Constitution: A Reader, would increase average gift since it was offered for any gift of $100 or more.
Since higher conversion rates and average gift compound to increase revenue, they thought this premium book offer could have a profound impact on revenue if successful. They launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: The page with the premium offer increased revenue by 53.8%. This was due to the compounding increases in conversion rate (26.2%) and average gift (21.9%). This reminds us that the three factors that make up revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average gift) all compound rather than add up. Simple addition would have led to a 48.1% increase. But the two happening in tandem led to a much more significant increase. This is a great reminder of the power of launching tests that could potentially affect multiple FCORM (Flux Capacitor of Online Revenue Maximization) metrics. (See https://www.nextafter.com/research/2017/02/how-adding-a-premium-book-offer-affects-donor-revenue/)

experiments.title: How the recurring gift popup impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a recurring giving popup increase recurring donors without negatively impacting one-time gifts?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/16/2019 - 09/07/2019
Hillsdale College wanted to find a way to increase donors to their recurring giving club named Liberty & Learning Society. In experiments run with similar organizations, we had found that showing a popup prompting for a recurring gift could increase the number of donors making a recurring gift. The popup was shown when a potential donor submits the donation form; it effectively interrupted the giving process in an attempt to position a different offer. For those organizations, we had also seen that the popup had no significant impact on one-time donor conversion. With this in mind, we decided to test this tactic on the primary and PPC donation forms for the college.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After relaunching this experiment, we found that the recurring giving popup decreased overall giving by 19%. We also observed that it decreased recurring gifts by 70%. The decrease in recurring was such a large decrease primarily due to a smaller number of conversions (13 vs. 4 gifts). The one-time donor decrease was seen across all of the various segments (mobile / desktop, new visitor / returning) and both pages.
It is our hypothesis that the primary reason behind the decrease is that people clicking on the donation button assumed they were finished when they saw the popup (due to not reading the actual text on the popup) which led them to "abandon" the giving process. This is different than what was observed with similar organizations which emphasizes the need to validate learnings seen with other organizations.

experiments.title: How a recurring gift prompt at fiscal year end impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a popup shown at the point of conversion increase recurring giving acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2019 - 07/31/2019
As a means to increase membership to Hillsdale’s Liberty and Learning Society (recurring giving club) we decided to launch an experiment where visitors making a one-time donation would be prompted with a popup after hitting “Submit.” This popup would encourage them to convert their one time gift to a recurring gift in order to have a greater impact and to receive a few of the benefits.
This experiment was launched solely on the pay per click primary donation page to minimize the potential negative impact that could come from the increased friction. Any donor making a gift above $500 would also be excluded from seeing the popup.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for nearly two months, we saw that the recurring giving prompt resulted in a decrease in overall conversion of 16%. It also didn't significantly impact recurring giving either positively or negatively. What made this surprising is that this same experiment had seen 30% increase in recurring giving with no negative impact on one-time gifts with a similar organization. Based upon this observation, we spent a significant amount of time analyzing the results.
During our analysis, we discovered that the recurring gift popup exhibited two different behaviors at the beginning and end of the experiment. Specifically, when we compared June to July, we found that June saw the decrease in conversion while July saw no significant impact. We also saw a slight boost in recurring giving in July.
It would appear that the urgency that comes with a fiscal year end increased the perceived friction of this experiment. During times of lower urgency, like July, the popup seemed to have the intended effect of increasing recurring giving. To further validate this hypothesis, we will relaunch the experiment and expand the pages targeted to shorten the testing cycle.

Because of the success of previous experiments that involved increasing the force and clarity of the value proposition, they hypothesized that the treatment page would receive more conversions.

Each of these possible reasons for decrease in conversion can be tested against the control to find out which, if any, can make a difference.

Second, the black on white text has been proven to increase reading difficulty, which might have contributed to the drop. This test was insightful as it highlighted factors that might need to be enhanced in the control to increase conversion.

experiments.title: How email engagement influence social media response rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which audience will result in the best return on investment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2017 - 05/31/2017
Hillsdale College was promoting the launch of their new DVD version of their popular Constitution 101 course. They had sent an email to their house file and wanted to see if they could raise more money by remarketing to that audience. Using a new technology named LeadsBridge, they created two distinct custom audiences in Facebook: one of subscribers that had clicked on the email and another of those that had simply opened.
Using these two distinct audiences, they then served them the same Facebook ad with a direct ask for a gift in order to receive the DVD set.
They closely monitored results to see which group was more likely to convert, and therefore should receive more remarketing dollars.
experiments.keyLearnings: The audience that had simply opened the email showed a 275% increase in response rate over those that had clicked. This flouts conventional wisdom that we should remarket to the audience that advanced the furthest down the marketing funnel—in this case, they might have seen the offer and decided it wasn’t for them. This is important to keep in mind when evaluating the success of your email program. Opens are important—and their lack of action right away should not rule them out for future conversion.

experiments.title: How different types of digital advertising content affects direct mail revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will different types of digital advertising content affect direct mail revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2018 - 01/31/2018
Hillsdale College had run an insightful experiment during the summer that showed that online advertising could have a significant impact on direct mail results. They wanted to run a subsequent test to accomplish two objectives. First, they wanted to see how the same principle affected a different package at a different time of year. Second, they wanted to see if showing different types of content affected revenue. To accomplish this objective, they created five unique segments: a control, that would see no ads, and four segments that would see Imprimis ads, Online Courses ads, Brand ads, and a mix of ads, respectively.
They set up each ad group and segment and showed ads one week before the mail packages hit homes and two weeks after to determine the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Only one of the groups showed any difference in significance—the online courses group, which showed a 7.5% decline in revenue. Overall, the results were a big change from what the earlier test had shown. There are a few potential hypotheses as to this outcome:
1. It is difficult to affect results when motivation is higher. Therefore, the package that dropped closer to year-end had an overall higher average conversion rate, and thus the ads did not make much of an impact.2. The online courses segment suffered in revenue because people signed up for (and contributed to) online courses, thus reducing their propensity to give.3. The content of the direct mail piece was stronger, which led to a higher conversion rate and less variance between the packages.
This shows that this test should be replicated with different direct mail pieces at different times of year and with different content—especially acquisition mailings.

experiments.title: How “cost reduction” value proposition affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a “cost reduction” value proposition affect recurring donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2019 - 04/23/2019
Hillsdale College sent a chase email as a follow up to a direct mail piece that asked donors to become a recurring donor as a part of their Liberty & Learning Society. The direct mail piece indicated that, as a member of this Society, donors would receive less direct mail and thus help Hillsdale reduce fundraising costs. They wanted to understand the impact of this “cost reduction” value proposition as opposed to a “benefits-focused” value proposition. They crafted two emails—one which included the “cost reduction and reduced direct mail” value proposition, and one which focused on the benefits of joining the LLS.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment, which removed the "cost reduction" value proposition as well as the promise to send less direct mail, produced a 55% decrease in overall revenue due to the combination of a decrease in traffic and a decrease in conversion rate. This shows two important learnings—first, that there may be a desire among donors to help Hillsdale be more efficient and reduce costs, and second, that donors who receive direct mail may have a desire to receive less of it. This learning can be refined in future testing—especially the dynamic between the two.

experiments.keyLearnings:
As predicted, the treatment subject line, was effective in achieving a higher open rate than the control subject line. In fact, the open rate for the treatment was 2.03% higher than the control. The treatment contained personalization, a synonym for the word “free,” and a call to action instead of a question. The combination of all of these resulted in a higher likelihood of the receiver opening the email.
The main purpose of the experiment was to measure the difference in open rates between the two subject lines, and those results were conclusive. However, the control subject line did achieve a higher click-through rate than the treatment. Looking into why this was the case could be the basis for future research.

experiments.title: How adding a photo to a landing page impacts donations

Hillsdale College recently mailed out thousands of letters to school administrators and teachers across America. In these letters, they offered to send a free pocket-sized copy of the U.S. Constitution for every single child in every classroom. Hillsdale received an astonishing number of replies requesting copies of the Constitution. To fund the shipment to all of these schools, Hillsdale launched a four-day email campaign asking donors to send a donation to help send pocket-sized Constitutions to schoolchildren across the U.S.
After sending the first two emails out to a split landing page test, where they tested the presence of gift handles that explained what each suggested gift amount would be used for, they concluded that adding just one line of copy around the gift handles did not increase likelihood of giving.
The Hillsdale team wondered about including a photo of some of the students who received Constitutions onto the landing page. Rather than telling the prospective donor about what they were doing, they could show them with a photo. Would adding a photo of schoolchildren receiving the Constitution increase donations? They hypothesized that adding the photo would show proof, causing an increase in trust, and would increase emotional appeal. They hypothesized that each of these would increase donations. This experiment was tested on their non-donor file.
experiments.keyLearnings: Because this experiment only ran for one day, the experiment results shouldn’t be rolled out to the rest of the donation pages. However, this result was enough for the Hillsdale team to roll out to this landing page for the duration of the campaign.

experiments.title: How reducing friction through a two-step form increases email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will simplifying the signup process through a two-step form increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2017 - 03/06/2017
Hillsdale College had previously verified that a two-step signup form for their online courses increased email acquisition rate. However, the platform on which they had built this two-step form made it difficult to do further testing on the signup process. So they decided to rebuild the two-step form on a new platform that made future testing easier.
They wanted to re-test the concept to make sure that the lift still held—and they also wanted to test a new concept. The previous winning treatment had two unique pages that each contained a part of the full form. Their new concept animated the two halves of the form on a single page in a smooth motion that didn’t require a second page.
They launched a three-way split URL test to validate a winning treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both treatments produced a lift to the control, as expected (since this had validated in a previous test on a different platform). However, the two-page solution produced a much higher lift than the “smooth animation” form—and also produced a 76% increase in donations after the signup process.
This was a fascinating result, since the two-page solution required more time and presented more friction. This result suggests a few potential learnings:
Enrollees might be more invested in the process with a more tedious signup process—in this case, more friction could have been a qualifying factor.
Older enrollees might have been confused by the “smooth animation” form, and the two-page solution provided more clarity.
The second page that asked for a home address provided a layer of insulation from poor donation prospects, as they might not have completed the entire process.
This treatment was quickly rolled out to all Hillsdale courses, and provided lifts across the board. The new two-step form also now allows more iterative testing for each course.

experiments.title: How assumption of desire for an offer affects open rate

The first approach asked a question—”Do you want a free pocket Constitution?” The second assumed that they did want it, and offered them to them: “Get your complimentary pocket Constitution”. experiments.keyLearnings: The subject line that assumed the recipient wanted the offer produced a 30% lift in open rate. There are two possible reasons for this. First, the question format allowed the recipient to decide whether or not to open the email before they even opened the email. If they didn't want it, many people may have just archived or deleted the email. Second, the two subject lines also used two different words to describe the offer: "free" and "complimentary". This may have had an impact, as "free" implies that the offer is widely available and of limited value, while "complimentary" implies that the offer is of some value but is given free of charge due to a relationship. This element should be specifically targeted to determine impact in a future test.

experiments.title: How refining a post-course donation page value proposition affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will refining a donation page’s value proposition affect revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/02/2019 - 01/12/2019
Hillsdale College had run multiple experiments on their post-course-donation pages, leading to a control that had not been beaten in some time. The control answered a question placed in the reader’s mind—why does Hillsdale teach these courses for free? It then went on to explain how Hillsdale does all these courses without funding from the government and asked the reader to support the College.
They decided to craft a new donation page value proposition that incorporated several new elements that they hypothesized would increase conversion. First they created a new headline that excited the reader, rather than simply thanking them. Then, they repositioned the following copy to position it as “must read” content, numbering the items so that the reader had some direction as to what order they were supposed to read in.
They kept the same belief statement first, but made a distinction in how they approached discussing the funding of the courses. Instead of mentioning the lack of government funding, they positioned the course as a gift to the donor, provided by other generous donors. This introduced the concept of reciprocity—that people are more likely to give if something is given to them first. The third point reminded the reader that they were furthering their education, and expressed gratitude. Finally, they concluded by giving a mission to the reader—help reach the “millions more” who don’t know about these courses. They reiterated the important nature of these courses, and then made an assertion: “We know exactly how much it costs to get a student enrolled in an online course. When you make a gift of $75 today, you will allow 15 more people to hear about this course.”
This communicates a few things to the reader:
Hillsdale is prudent and strategic in marketing these courses
The reader can have a defined impact with a defined donation amount—giving them a handle.
They launched the treatment in an A/B test and watched results to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 127% lift in revenue, due to an 88.1% increase in conversion and 21% increase in average gift. Not only did this new copy inspire more people to read and subsequently give, they were inspired to give more. Further testing is required to know if this average gift increase is due to the copy or to the suggested $75 gift handle, but these results are now being tested across multiple courses to see if the results hold.

experiments.title: How a simpler “minor mystery” affects clickthrough rate to a video from email
experiments.researchQuestion: How will simplifying the “minor mystery” affect clickthrough rate to a video from email?

Hillsdale College had produced a video highlighting the fact that they receive no funding from the government—”Not One Penny”. They were sending this video to their house file as cultivation for the calendar year-end campaign, and wanted as many people to see it as possible. They had a control from a previous send that asked the question: “Why is it so important for Hillsdale to remain independent”? and pushed users to the video to find out. They wanted to get more traffic to the video, so they decided to create a test treatment.
The control won in previous tests because it leveraged the “minor mystery”—creating tension that could be resolved (or answered, in this case) by clicking through. But upon further review, the “minor mystery” assumed that the reader wanted to know “why”—which is a tall order. They hypothesized that they could increase clickthrough rate by lowering the barrier to the minor mystery. Instead of “why”, they would ask “what”. They crafted a treatment that asked a much simpler question—”How much money does Hillsdale College receive from the government?”
They split the traffic in a test segment to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test treatment, with the "lower hurdle" of "what" versus the control's "why" produced a 45.5% increase in clickthrough rate. This shows that we might be able to attract a larger audience with a simpler-to-understand concept. While many may latch on to the "why" behind what Hillsdale does, many more will click through to answer the simple "how much" question. And in this case, with traffic as the goal, more clicks wins.

Typically this would be an interesting, but not insightful learning, except for the fact that it was contradicted by another test on Hillsdale's online courses page in which the "launch" language won. This shows the importance of testing across multiple properties with different messages so that you can match the right message to the right audience.

experiments.title: How a personalized note affects clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad

Hillsdale College’s course, “An Introduction to C.S. Lewis: Writings and Significance” has remained one of their most popular courses over the last few years. After receiving a significant amount of positive feedback, they found that many people wanted to own a DVD version of this course. So they created a DVD Box Set and began to offer it to their fans online. As they began this new campaign, they wondered: Will a personal note from Dr. Larry Arnn increase the motivation and clickthrough rate of the ad viewer? They created a treatment and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale College found that the personal note treatment version decreased clickthrough rate by 12.5%. There are a couple of possibilities for this slight decrease. First, it’s possible that this treatment version actually decreased the clarity of the offer. While it did add more context to why the DVD box set was being offered, it did not explicitly mention the DVD box set. Second, it’s also possible that this message from Dr. Arnn increased the anxiety of the ad viewers. They may have hesitated and wondered if Dr. Arnn really did say those words.
While these results are valid, this test has prompted further studies with different types of offers to further prove if a personalized note increases or decreases clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad.

The donation form for Hillsdale has gone through a series of iterations to land at its current state. The form is not highly styled but it is laid out in such a way that we felt it would be easiest for donors to fill out. With the recent shifts taking place online with regards to user experience, we realized that the form was not as modern looking as many of the other options out there.
We decided to try improving the user experience by giving the form some more modern touches. This included minimizing the visual size of the form by removing the labels from above the fields. Then, so that users would know what information was required for each field, we implemented a technique known as adaptive placeholders. Adaptive placeholders sit in the field until a user focuses on the field, at which point the placeholder floats up above the field. You can see this form in action here.
We then tested this new form against the our original control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment result was heartbreaking. We were so certain that the more modern form would create a much better user experience which would lead to more donations. At a minimum, we expected it to have no significant impact. We never thought it would so dramatically reduce the conversion rate of the page!

Our hypothesis for this result is that the new user experience was a distraction for donor. It was far different than they were use to which made it more work to give a gift. These two factors ultimately led to abandonment of the donation process which reduced conversion rate by 69.7%

experiments.title: How placement of a thermometer affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will higher thermometer placement increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2016 - 07/05/2016
Hillsdale College was nearing the end of their fiscal year-end fundraising campaign. Their email series included several “hard asks” in the email, or email content that asked for the donation, rather than simply trying to get readers to click through. This led to lower clickthrough rates and higher conversion rates.
They decided to try to incentivize users who had not clicked through yet to do so by inviting them to “come see the progress” made towards the goal. However, the thermometer that showed the progress was further down the page, since previous testing had shown that presenting the thermometer in proximity to the paragraph that stated the end date improved conversion.
Hillsdale hypothesized that moving the thermometer to the top of the page would instantly show users the gap between the goal and the amount raised, and would increase their motivation to help meet it.
They launched an A/B test to determine the most effective placement of the thermometer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The higher thermometer placement produced a 19.8% decrease in conversion on the donation page. This could tell us a few things:
That moving the thermometer to the top gave the “payoff” of the email away. People clicked through just to see progress, and since they were able to instantly see that in the treatment, they didn’t engage with the page.
The thermometer adds the most value when it is closest to the paragraph that mentions the deadline. This is basic contextualization—any page element performs better in context than out of context.
This also reminds us that the thermometer is just a device—it doesn’t convey value proposition on its own. That task is left to the copy, and if we want our readers to absorb the value proposition, we must engage them with the copy.

experiments.title: How a stronger value statement in a headline affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a broader value statement in the headline increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2016 - 05/03/2016
Hillsdale College was promoting their free Constitution 101 course through multiple online channels. The control signup page converted nearly 40% of visitors to enroll in the course, but it also assumed that the primary value proposition, “Understand the Constitution like never before” resonated with each visitor.
They wanted to test whether a broader statement might capture the attention of more people who did not already have an inclination to “understand the Constitution like never before” and convince more to enroll. They also hypothesized that a “value statement” in the headline might result in more donations. So they created a second page, with the headline “The Constitution secures your freedoms as an American” and the subheadline “Understand it like never before in Hillsdale College’s free online course”.
They launched an A/B test to determine the winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the new page with the broader headline actually decreased email acquisition conversion rate by 2.7%, we noticed that visitors to this page were 14.4% more likely to donate.
This presented an interesting finding: while the broader statement resulted in fewer course enrollments, it increased the motivation to give for those who did enroll. This seems to suggest that while the new headline was more divisive among initial visitors (which caused slightly lower enrollment), it increased the appeal of the course by playing to the values (rather than just the surface desires) of those who accepted the offer. This reiterates the importance of measuring the entire funnel when there is both email acquisition and donor acquisition at stake. It also shows that increasing the force of your value proposition means accepting trade-offs. An effective value proposition will not appeal to everyone.

experiments.title: How a signature image in an email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing a signature increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2016 - 10/01/2016
Hillsdale College was announcing their new course about the Supreme Court. For years, they had included a picture of their President’s signature in each email, much like their direct mail letters.
But they hypothesized that this image might decrease the credibility of the email, since people don’t traditionally sign emails.
They decided to quantify the impact of the visual “signature” by creating a treatment that removed the image and left the plain-text signature. They measured clickthrough rate as the effective metric to determine trust.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, without the signature, produced a 12.9% lift in clickthrough rate. This indicates that the signature had a slight effect on reducing the recipient’s trust of the sender, which resulted in fewer recipients taking action.
The signature image is a good example of a skeumorphic device, which is an ornamental design feature retained from previous design—in this case, a direct mail letter.
While skeumorphic devices can be helpful initially to maintain visual continuity for users, they can also reduce the “native” appearance and increase perception that the communication is not really from a person.

experiments.title: How the force of the value proposition affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the force of the value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2016 - 06/29/2016
Hillsdale College was in the middle of their fiscal year-end fundraising campaign when they decided to test a new framework for developing copy on their donation page. Their current donation page started with the headline “You can help defend the Constitution and restore liberty”, which told the reader what they could do. The copy then led into the three points that Hillsdale would do (with their donation) to accomplish these objectives.
But Hillsdale hypothesized that the page was missing a real problem to draw the reader in and set up the three initiatives of the campaign. Additionally, the transitions between each of the initiatives — the glue that draws them together — were missing. They drafted a treatment that started with the problem: “Our Country Stands at a Constitutional Crossroads”. Then, they developed copy that moved seamlessly between the initiatives and called out the fact that Hillsdale receives no government funding, even in the form of student loans.
They launched an A/B copy test on the page, and tested it as they sent multiple email appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new copy produced an 18.4% lift in donor conversion, with a directional increase in average gift as well.
This shows that the new framework for donation page copy and presentation of the value proposition may increase fundraising results. While this was just one campaign, it certainly makes the case for continued testing of this copy model in future campaigns.
This experiment also shows the importance of frameworks. Without a framework, or a hypothesis behind why the copy was changed, we might just conclude that one landing page was “just better than the other”. But the framework tells us exactly what elements were changed, strengthened, and re-ordered to create better-performing copy with a stronger value proposition. This framework ensures that the result can be replicated on future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a more personalized value proposition affects remarketing conversion rate

Hillsdale College decided to launch remarketing ads on Facebook to users who visited the signup page for their acclaimed “Constitution 101” course. They deliberated about the copy on these remarketing ads—should it re-state the original value proposition of the course, or should it encourage them to finish completion of the course?
Since this was a testable hypothesis, they created two ads. The first told the user that their course was still available, and encouraged them to finish signup. The second used a standard value proposition that encouraged them to grow their understanding of the Constitution through the course, but didn’t show any indicator that they had shown previous interest.

The treatment produced a 393% lift in conversion rate, which reveals a few key learnings about user behavior and remarketing ads. First it shows that reminding people that they failed to complete signup might make them feel guilty and less likely to sign up. Second, it reveals that this is remarketing, and could possibly indicate a “creepiness” factor that they are being followed around the web.
But most importantly, it shows the power of removing the true value proposition from an ad—even an remarketing ad—and the concurrent effect that it has on motivation. Even if a user has already absorbed the value proposition from a previous ad, remarketing ads must contain a strong value proposition as well if they are to complete conversion the second time around.

experiments.title: How social influence impacted the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will social validation (the number of people that have taken the course) be a significant motivator to increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2016 - 12/16/2016
Hillsdale College has had nearly one million people take their Constitution 101 course that is offered for free. This offer serves as one of the primary acquisition sources for both emails and donors for the college. As a result, any increases to conversion has a significant impact on the college.
In our efforts to boost overall email acquisition, we wanted to see if we could use the popularity of the course to help sell the value to the visitor. To accomplish this, we created an animated counter on the page that would go from zero to over 800K. It would then increment by two more over the span of another minute. Our goal was to use social influence represented by the volume of people that have taken the course and then the additional number added would reinforce that it is still active.

experiments.title: How reducing the specificity of an offer affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will reducing the specificity of an offer (in favor of a big vision) affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2016 - 09/12/2016
Hillsdale College was raising money in honor of Constitution Day, a holiday to mark the day the Constitution was signed. They had a new project they were excited about, The National Index of Constitutional Literacy. They created this initiative to measure the effectiveness of their popular online courses that teach Constitutional principles. While the project was specific, they wondered if it was too specific, and if a broader, “big vision” appeal might attract more interest from their file. They decided to create a treatment and let the data direct the general value proposition of their overall campaign.
They crafted a second appeal that included the Index of Constitutional Literacy, but as part of a big vision meant to reverse the marginalization of the Constitution in America. They split their file evenly and launched the two appeals, selecting clickthrough rate as the initial indicator of donor interest.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the bigger vision, produced an significant increase in clickthrough rate—788.4%. This showed that Hillsdale donors, while interested in the project, showed much more interest in an initiative that was far-reaching in scope and much larger in impact. However, clickthrough rate is not the only metric at play here. If more people don’t give to the larger vision, then this experiment produces no actionable learning.
The team dug deeper into the data to find how reducing the specificity of the offer affected giving.

experiments.title: How donation page imagery affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including the book in the offer image increase the average gift?
experiments.summary:

Hillsdale College offered their popular course “Constitution 101” on DVD as a premium for a donation of $100. So they put together a page and offered it to people who had signed up for the course as an opportunity to get a physical copy of the course material. The first offering to the house file was a huge success. However, they wondered if they could attract more visitors to upgrade to the higher offer, which offered the DVD plus their Constitution Reader book for a gift of $200.
One thing they noticed on the page was that the image only showed the DVD, since it was the featured premium in the copy. They hypothesized that, by highlighting the “combo” premium package including the book in the image, they could increase average gift.
They send out a second send to determine a winner.

This experiment shows the power of the visual image and how it anchors desire for the visitor, convincing visitors to double their gift to receive twice as much. This will lead to testing upgrade offers on all of Hillsdale’s courses, and wherever a premium is featured.

experiments.title: How copy length affects the value proposition of a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the length and depth of the value proposition through the ad copy attract more people to click on the ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2017 - 02/08/2017
Hillsdale College launched new creative on Facebook promoting their course on the life of C.S. Lewis. Since these ads are direct response ads designed to attract viewers to sign up for the course, they had previously experienced much success with concise, clear copy that asked people to sign up.
However, they felt that they might have saturated the market of people who needed minimal persuasion to sign up, and hypothesized that to engage the next, less-motivated layer of the target audience, their ads might actually need more copy. They crafted copy for the top section of the ad that gave more insight into who C.S. Lewis was, and made a softer offer to sign up for the course.
They launched an A/B split test with the ads to determine a winner, knowing that Facebook would direct more traffic towards the ad that performed better.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new copy produced a 421.6% increase in clickthrough rate. This seemed to validate that the target audience (at this point in the campaign) needed more copy to convince them to take the course, but there were a few other factors at play as well:
The new copy took up more vertical space, which captured a larger percentage of the Facebook newsfeed and drew the eye to the ad.
The increased length of the copy drew the eye to the ad and gave it the perception of having greater substance, which attracted more readers to engage with the copy.
The more inviting tone of the ad copy appealed to readers and drew them into the ad, rather than forcing them to make a quick decision about whether or not they wanted to sign up for a course that they didn’t know much about.
This learning provides immense opportunities for testing new ad creative across the board with all of Hillsdale’s courses, especially those that have run for an extended period of time.

experiments.title: How natural urgency affects the clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emphasizing the natural urgency of a limited-time offer increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2017 - 04/21/2017
After successfully launching their most popular online course, “Constitution-101,” Hillsdale College decided to offer a limited-time only DVD Set version. This was designed for those who enjoyed the course but also wanted to physically own and share it with others. They used this product launch to test whether the natural urgency of the limited-time offer affected the targeted audience’s decision to take action. Using the emotive appeal “Bring Constitution 101 to your living room” as a control, they created a treatment with more direct, urgent language.
Keeping all other elements the same, they launched this A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment group that emphasized the natural urgency of the limited-time offer produced a 17.6% increase over the control group. This experiment validated Hillsdale College’s hypothesis that their target audience is more inclined to take action when there is an apparent sense of urgency. This is a valuable discovery about the motivation triggers present in the mind of potential donors—one that Hillsdale College can use across other courses.
Whether we realize it or not, most of our daily actions are driven by factors of urgency. Our digital behavior is no exception. That knowledge is powerful. However, it does not permit organizations to manipulate audiences by creating a false sense of urgency. These attempts are easily sniffed out and will only harm the reputation of the organization. But using natural urgency to alert an audience, as we have seen here, can be an effective way to motivate action.

experiments.title: How emotive headline copy affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emotive headline copy produce an increase in email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2017 - 03/31/2017
Hillsdale College launched an updated version of their popular history course, American Heritage. They had received feedback on the original version of the course from many subscribers that the course “taught history the way it should be taught in school.” They decided to test whether this was a value proposition that resonated with others by crafting a similar headline: “Learn the history of America—the way it should be taught in school.” However, they feared that this might be too negative of a headline—and too focused on what is not taught in school, rather than what is taught in the course. So they created a treatment with highly emotive language: “Deepen your understanding of the incredible story of American Liberty.”
They kept all the other elements on the page the same, and launched an A/B headline test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment headline with the emotive language produced a 168.7% lift over the headline related to what is not taught in schools. This produced a valuable learning about motivation to enroll in the free course: we must orient our language around what the course does for the user, rather than align with a belief (even if it is true) about what the course replaces in the public sphere.
When a visitor comes to the page from an ad, they are motivated to do something, and our language must continue that momentum rather than causing their mind to drift elsewhere. Even if they align with the value proposition, a headline that doesn’t focus on what the course will do for them can mentally distract them from the ultimate goal of enrollment.

experiments.title: How content offer illustrations impact email acquisition rate in a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the “Introduction to the Constitution” course illustration attract more people than an actual image of the course setting?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2017 - 05/23/2017
Hillsdale College decided to take a new approach to producing one of their most popular online courses: Introduction to the Constitution. Instead of showing the teacher lecturing from a podium in a classroom setting, they presented the teacher—in this case, Dr. Larry Arnn—sitting at a table discussing the material with his students. This groundbreaking new style immerses the viewer in the Hillsdale classroom experience.
Since this was a completely different approach than before, they were interested in learning how the general public would respond to the change. To discover whether this “insider” view of the classroom would resonate with their audience, they launched a Facebook A/B test to determine what type of imagery would increase course signups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale found that the course illustration produced an 85.5% increase in email acquisition, and thus, course signups. Though the new approach to producing this course has resonated well overall with Hillsdale College’s target audience, it’s apparent that showing a “quick glance” of the course in action via a Facebook ad was not well received. It’s clear that the course illustration produced a higher level of intrigue, amounting in not only a higher clickthrough rate but also a higher email acquisition rate.
Hillsdale’s audience may have shied away from the image of the course because it was radically different than previous course advertisements. The “quick glance” may have, in fact, caused more confusion about the value proposition and added to the anxiety of the viewer. These results have prompted further testing across other courses to determine the importance of course illustrations in Facebook advertising.

experiments.title: How perceived value, clarity, and congruence affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will aligning the image of the offer with the details of the offer increase conversion?

Hillsdale College was offering a single-issue download of their popular publication Imprimis. They had typically offered Imprimis as a full subscription, and had used an image of a stack of issues, with the most recent on top. However, they wondered if this “multi-issue” image was making the actual “single-issue” offer unclear. This could be a risk, as showing multiple issues might increase the perceived value of the offer. They created an identical ad with a single issue and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which aligned the image with the offer, produced a 52.7% increase in conversion, though there was no discernible increase in clickthrough rate. This revealed an interesting learning—although the single-issue version didn’t increase the likelihood to click (meaning that it didn’t increase perceived clarity at the initial point of contact), it had a big increase on the likelihood to convert by aligning and increasing clarity at the point of decision.
This is a great example of how experiments must be measured along the entire funnel. If Hillsdale just measured an advertising experiment at the advertising level, then they would not make any changes. However, there were significant effects downstream—only discovered by measuring the entire funnel.

experiments.title: How an intentional focal point affects email acquisition rate on a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an intentional focal point increase clickthrough and email acquisition rate in a Facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2017 - 08/18/2017
Hillsdale College reviewed the Facebook ad creative for their online course “Winston Churchill and Statesmanship,” and found that their ad clickthrough rate was below the normal threshold. They wondered: Will a more focused Facebook ad image increase both clickthrough and email acquisition rate? They created a second version of their Winston Churchill Facebook ad that (1) added a lighter color to the background, (2) blurred the background and (3) increased the size of the supporting ad text. They set this treatment against the control and began to monitor the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale College found that the treatment version significantly increased clickthrough rate, while increasing email acquisition rate by 37%. It’s clear that version B decreased the anxiety of the viewer and increased their motivation even beyond the initial ad click. Why? There’s a couple of potential reasons for this increase.
First, it’s likely that the ad text in our control version was too small for the average set of eyes to read without additional strain. The treatment version increases the size of the ad text significantly. Second, the background in the control version is not blurred out, which causes the reader to more friction. The treatment version, on the other hand, focus the viewer’s attention on Winston Churchill and “Activate Your Free Course Now!” This signals the reader to the two most important takeaways, without additional work. Finally, the control version shows an intense (and perhaps, less than desirable?) image of Churchill. This may cause unwanted anxiety for the viewer.
Hillsdale College has used this test to develop new tests and audit their other existing ads.

On their “Economics 101” email-specific course signup page, Hillsdale College converts visitors at a rate of 32%. After reexamining, they noticed the value proposition found on this page lacked incentive and a clear answer to the question, “So what?” They wondered if visitors needed a clearer answer to “why” taking this course was important right now, as a citizen of the United States. Hillsdale College found that this audience values upholding the duty of being a good and informed U.S. citizen, in so much that they are more willing to take this course when it is presented as an avenue to achieve that.
This also underscores the importance of using relevance as a tool to create greater clarity and connection to an offer.
This is important for Hillsdale to consider. Their independence from government aid and specific educational mission gives them a unique platform and credibility with their audience. In other words, a recommendation from Hillsdale College on civic duty goes a long way in the eyes of many Americans. In light of this finding, they are conducting similar tests across other courses.

experiments.title: How incentive and 3rd party credibility affects conversion rate

The new treatment outperformed the contextualized donation page by 230%.  This proved the incentive behind the matching gift language and increased credibility outweighed the specificity of associating the gift with their previous action.

They launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By adding the image of Winston Churchill and his name to the headline, a greater number of people were able to quickly understand the core value proposition of the content. Without the additional content, visitors were forced to guess which world leader the content was addressing, even though it is stated immediately in the body copy.
This test also reminds us of the importance of the headline. Winston’s name is the first two words of the body copy, yet, many people did not stay on the page long enough to make the connection.
Finally, as Hillsdale continues to test across multiple channels, they are quickly finding that what is true for one acquisition pathway is not always true for the others. This is an important thing to keep in mind during the ongoing optimization process because it reminds us that not all site traffic is motivated in the same way or by the same thing.

experiments.title: How a Tailored, Remarketing Headline Affects Clickthrough Rate on a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Facebook headline that emphasizes beginning the first class in an online course increase clickthrough rate?

Timeframe: 08/28/2017 - 09/06/2017
Hillsdale College recently launched remarketing Facebook audiences, targeting individuals who viewed their course signup pages. They wondered if tailoring the Facebook headline to these visitors would increase clickthrough rate. They created a treatment with the headline “Begin your first class now »” and tested it against their control headline “Activate your free course now »”. They monitored this test for a little over a week and documented the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale College found that the treatment version of this Facebook ad actually decreased clickthrough rate by 72%. There are couple of potential reasons for this sizable decrease in clickthrough.
First, it’s possible that the remarketing audience did not find the headline call-to-action as persuasive or relevant as the control version. “Begin your first class now»” may have increased anxiety in the viewer, who felt they didn’t have the time to take the first class then and there. Second, this remarketing audience already left the signup page for a reason, without signing up. It is possible that they have a lower motivation to clickthrough on an offer they have already been exposed to. This insight has prompted a similar remarketing experiment, testing for “same image vs. different image.”
Finally, this headline may have also not been specific enough to the remarketing audience. Further testing will be done with more pointed headlines, such as “Return and Activate your course now»”.

This means that some visitors had to scroll down the page to read the value proposition language surrounding Hillsdale’s free online courses.
