experiments.title: How subject line affects name acquisition conversion rate

rows.name: Get Your Free Pocket Constitution Now
Free Pocket Constitution, courtesy of Hillsdale College

experiments.researchQuestion: Do the treatment subject lines show a significant improvement in name acquisition conversion over the control subject line, not only for opens, but also for overall conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/05/2016 - 10/06/2016
Hillsdale College sent prospecting emails to a Townhall Media email file to generate signups for its free Pocket Constitution offer. Our hypothesis was that subject line could effect open rate, but that by clarifying the value proposition, the subject line could also drive a more motivated and qualified reader to the landing page, resulting in higher conversion rates.
The file was randomized into four segments, with each segment receiving the control or one of the three treatment versions. The subject line was the only variable changed between each test. All other email elements and landing pages were identical.
The subject lines used were:
+Control: Keep this in your pocket
+Treatment#1: Keep THIS in Your Pocket
+Treatment#2: Get Your Free Pocket Constitution Now
+Treatment#3: Free Pocket Constitution, courtesy of Hillsdale College
In this case, Samples count is the number of emails sent, and emails acquired is the total number of people who completed the signup process on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: Sometimes more ambiguous subject lines can tease a reader into opening an email. In this case, explicitly stating the value proposition at the front of the subject line drove higher clicks and higher conversions. This is the case when the value proposition is very direct and simple to understand (“Free Pocket Constitution”). experiments.keyLearnings:
Note: a subsequent experiment proved these results to be invalid due to instrumentation error.
In the second iteration of experiments on the survey to Imprimis subscribers, the new treatment resulted in a 39% decrease to survey responses. This was similar to the results of the first experiment which answered our question whether the drop in responses was caused by the value proposition language at the top of the form (it was not). This led us to conclude that our efforts to reduce the friction of the form may have actually increased it.
In addition to moving the donation form to the second screen and adding conditional logic, we also combined multiple questions into a matrix format. The belief was that the condensed form would have less visual friction but the it would seem the format was confusing to many visitors which led to form abandonment.
On the positive side, the condensed form saw only a 9% decrease in donations (which is impressive considering the 39% decrease in traffic) and a 95% increase in average gift. If we can fix the survey responses, we can improve the overall donor conversion and revenue of the campaign.

But they wanted to better understand which calls to action drove the greatest amount of traffic to the page. They decided to test whether their audiences were actually motivated at that point in the funnel to “activate” — or if a softer call to action like “Learn More” would drive more traffic and conversions. By giving people the correct “micro-yes” with a “Learn More” CTA, we actually can pique their curiosity and enroll more students from the ads.

On Hillsdale College’s main donation form, we had done previous experiments to lift conversion rate 22% by modifying the format of the donation form and adding messaging around what we believed is both unique and appealing about Hillsdale College to potential donors. In an effort to continue optimizing this high traffic page, we shifted our focus to reducing friction and adding incentive to the gift.
We reduced friction by adding a lower starting donation amount to the gift array with the belief that a larger ask may make the donors feel a smaller gift would not be as impactful.  We also removed dividers between each section of the form to help move visitors down the page.  For incentive, we added the existing offer to receive a brick in the College’s Liberty Walk for a gift of $500 or more.

The new donation form treatment was able to produce a 32.5% increase in conversion and a lift to average gift of 13.8%. We noticed was that, despite adding the incentive for a $500+ gift, we didn’t see any donors give at or above that level so the incentive did not seem to have much impact on the average gift.

Our initial concern was that adding the $25 option to the gift array would lower the average gift enough to negate any increases in conversion. This proved to be false.  The lower gift was able to increase the conversion rate and it did not seem to make donors give less.

experiments.title: How an engagement device affects clickthrough rate versus a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a survey ask instead of a direct donation ask increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2016 - 08/29/2016
Hillsdale College wanted to send a digital follow-up to a direct mail appeal to a segment of their donors who had both a mailing address and an email address on file. These “chase” appeals had generated a significant amount of revenue in the past, but they wanted to try a new approach.
Previously, these emails just mirrored the direct mail package and made a direct ask for a donation. However, the direct mail package for their August appeal included a survey. They hypothesized that they could get better engagement by asking the donors to take a survey (that led to a donation) rather than putting a direct ask in the email. Ultimately, they hypothesized that this increase in clickthrough rate would result in more people reaching the donation page, and higher donor conversion. But first, they needed to get more clicks.
They recreated the survey online, split their email file, and send one segment a direct ask and the other a link to take a survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The survey, as expected, delivered a 693.1% increase in clickthrough rate. A soft ask almost always delivers more clicks than a direct donation ask, because the motivations are entirely different.
However, more clicks doesn’t always mean more donations, since people aren’t specifically clicking to make a donation. That warranted another test to see how the survey affected donor conversion.

				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How an engagement device affects donor conversion versus a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an engagement device (in this case, a survey) increase donor response and attract more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/16/2016 - 08/29/2016
Hillsdale College wanted to send a digital follow-up to a direct mail appeal to a segment of their donors who had both a mailing address and an email address on file. These “chase” appeals had generated a significant amount of revenue in the past, but they wanted to try a new approach.
Previously, these emails just mirrored the direct mail package and made a direct ask for a donation. However, the direct mail package for their August appeal included a survey. They hypothesized that they could get better engagement by asking the donors to take a survey (that led to a donation) rather than putting a direct ask in the email.
They recreated the survey online, split their email file, and send one segment a direct ask and the other a link to take a survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The survey produced a 126% lift in donations by attracting more recipients to start the donation process.
The survey email generated a 688% increase in clickthrough rate, although the Hillsdale team knew from experience that the survey ask was a far different motivation than the donor ask. However, this massive increase in clicks from the email got more people to start the process. Even after people fell out of the “funnel” as they went through the survey, the increase in traffic was significant enough to produce more than twice as many donations.
This inspired future guidance for chase emails. Although a survey every month would not be a sustainable strategy, using different engagement devices to get more people beginning the donation process proved to be a successful tactic.

				× 126.1% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How Facebook ad image text affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will more specific language in the image of a Facebook ad increase downstream email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2016 - 06/07/2016
Hillsdale College launched a free digital offer for their popular eBook Liberty and Learning, with a donation ask after the download was processed. As they created the ads for this offer, they wanted to test copy in the Facebook ad to determine how “ready to download” their traffic was.
The first ad assumed that people who saw the ads weren’t very convinced and needed to “learn more” first. This language was highlighted in the image, which is the most visually engaging part of a Facebook ad.
The second ad gave more context to the offer, explaining that it was a “free eBook”. While this gave more clarity to the offer, it also made an assumption that someone was “ready to download” when they clicked.
In the initial test, the treatment produced a lift in clickthrough rate, but Hillsdale wanted to look deeper into the data to make sure that those who clicked the ads were motivated to actually accept the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: On top of the 20.6% increase in clickthrough rate, the treatment ads produced a 26.8% conversion rate, which means that those who clicked the treatment ads were also more likely to convert. This second slice of data validates the learnings from the first experiment, and shows the importance of measuring the entire marketing funnel.

experiments.title: How Facebook ad image text affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will more specific language in the image of a Facebook ad increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2016 - 06/07/2016
Hillsdale College launched a free digital offer for their popular eBook Liberty and Learning, with a donation ask after the download was processed. As they created the ads for this offer, they wanted to test copy in the Facebook ad to determine how “ready to download” their traffic was.
The first ad assumed that people who saw the ads weren’t very convinced and needed to “learn more” first. This language was highlighted in the image, which is the most visually engaging part of a Facebook ad.
The second ad gave more context to the offer, explaining that it was a “free eBook”. While this gave more clarity to the offer, it also made an assumption that someone was “ready to download” when they clicked.
They launched an A/B test of ads to validate a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 20.6% increase in clickthrough rate, which showed that people who saw the ad were more likely to take initial action when presented with more specific language in the ad. This is aided be by Hillsdale’s precise targeting, but it also tells us that clarity around the offer entices more people to take the first step.
However, clickthrough rate might be deceiving, so Hillsdale measured conversion rate as well to make sure that the lift held all the way through the marketing funnel.

experiments.title: How length of copy affects clickthrough rate in a video email
experiments.researchQuestion: How much “selling” is needed to earn the click to watch a video?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2015 - 12/15/2015
Hillsdale College included a video soft-touch ask as part of their year-end fundraising campaign. This email was not designed to directly raise money, though there was a form on the page. It was meant to inspire readers to watch the video and prepare them for an ask later in the week. Therefore, it was critical to get as many people to the landing page as possible — which meant that Hillsdale needed to optimize the email’s clickthrough rate.
The original version of the email used lots of copy to “sell” the click. We developed a hypothesis that a video, when shown to a relevant audience, wouldn’t need nearly as much “selling” copy to win the click. We developed a treatment with dramatically less copy, moving the screenshot (which linked to the landing page) closer to the top of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test once again proves that clarity always trumps persuasion. If we can be straightforward about what we are wanting the user to do, it often outperforms a longer-form, more persuasive approach.

The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
26.9% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How personalization and brand reduction affect donor conversion on a post-signup donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: How will personalization and brand reduction affect donor conversion on a post-signup donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2020 - 09/05/2020
Hillsdale College was running a pre-registration campaign to promote their new course “Introduction to Western Philosophy”. They wanted to test two concepts on the donation page that followed the pre-registration. The first concept they wanted to test was personalization. Since the user signed up and gave their first name, they wanted to test a version of the donation page that congratulated the user by name for signing up. They were interested to see if this personalization would increase engagement on the page and humanize the “transaction”, resulting in an increased conversion rate. They also noticed that the approved copy had multiple repeated mentions of both Hillsdale and the full course name, “Introduction to Western Philosophy”. They hypothesized that removing these repeat mentions, replacing with “our” and “this course” would both “get the brand out of the way” and increase the readability of the page, resulting in increased conversion. Since they expected a large influx of traffic for this pre-registration period, they launched a three-way test to see if they could find a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both treatments had positive increases on the control, but in different ways.
Treatment one, with the added personalization, actually had very little impact on conversion but resulted in a statistically valid 26% lift in revenue. This indicates that personalization may increase the force of the value proposition in the mind of the donor, inspiring them to give more generously.
Treatment two, which removed the brand and course mentions, produced two increases, neither statistically valid but both directionally interesting. First, it increased conversion rate by 10.6% with a 86.9% LoC. While this did not validate, it did show a trend that, with more traffic, might be proven favorable. It also produced a 19% lift in revenue, with a 91% LoC—again, not statistically significant, but revealing a learning to be re-tested in a future campaign.
Hillsdale's team decided to combine the two treatments in a future offer to see if the combination of the personalization (which had a valid lift to revenue) and the reduction of brand terminology (which had a near-valid lift to conversion) would work together to produce a valid lift overall.

experiments.title: How personalization and brand reinforcement affect donor conversion on a post-signup donation page
How adding a photo to a landing page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a photo to a landing page increase the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/28/2019 - 03/13/2019
Hillsdale College recently mailed out thousands of letters to school administrators and teachers across America. In these letters, they offered to send a free pocket-sized copy of the U.S. Constitution for every single child in every classroom. Hillsdale received an astonishing number of replies requesting copies of the Constitution. To fund the shipment to all of these schools, Hillsdale launched a four-day email campaign asking donors to send a donation to help send pocket-sized Constitutions to schoolchildren across the U.S. After sending the first two emails out to a split landing page test, where they tested the presence of gift handles that explained what each suggested gift amount would be used for, they concluded that adding just one line of copy around the gift handles did not increase likelihood of giving.
The Hillsdale team wondered about including a photo of some of the students who received Constitutions onto the landing page. Rather than telling the donor about what they were doing, they could show them with a photo. Would adding a photo of schoolchildren receiving the Constitution increase donations? They hypothesized that adding the photo would show proof, causing an increase in trust, and would increase emotional appeal. They hypothesized that each of these would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a 15% lift from the treatment with the photo. Because this experiment only ran for one day, the experiment results shouldn’t be rolled out to the rest of the donation pages. However, this result was enough for the Hillsdale team to roll out to this landing page for the duration of the campaign.

experiments.title: How social pressure in the copy affects change in conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: Does pressuring rather than encouraging someone to improve their knowledge cause a change in conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2018 - 04/25/2018
This experiment was tested on Hillsdale College’s online course page. They wanted to know if copy that pressured the reader to engage would perform better than the more encouraging treatment, which asked the reader to enhance what they already know. The control makes the reader feel like they need to be better or face ridicule, while the treatment encourages the reader. The hypothesis was that the more direct language would pressure the reader to make the conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The copy that applied more pressure to the reader saw a .52% click rate while the more encouraging copy saw a .57% click rate. Since there was no statistically significant difference in results, we can conclude that the more direct copy had no change in conversion results.

experiments.title: How matching the visual congruence of the initial ad affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will simplifying and visually matching the original add improve email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2016 - 02/28/2016
As part of the launch and promotion of Hillsdale College’s latest free online course that studied the life and work of CS Lewis, we created an email capture page based upon what we had learned from prior experiments. This page utilized a design and copy similar to past online courses.
However, after reviewing the entire process, we realized that the default email capture form may cause a bit of disorientation for visitors coming from the ad. The ad (shown below) had a completely different look and feel than the landing page.
We had the hypothesis that if we can improve the visual congruence of the page and align the headline better to the call-to-action of the ad, we can increase the number of emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not a exponential lift, we did find that by visually aligning the design with the original ad we were able to increase email capture rate by 3.9%. This is a concept that will be valuable to utilize in future email acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the clarity and specificity of the value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/24/2015 - 12/04/2015
Hillsdale College launched a popular new course studying the life of Christian apologist C.S. Lewis. Donor conversion was low in comparison to other courses, so they investigated the copy on the donation page. The value proposition presented described Hillsdale’s online courses in general. They knew that this course attracted a lot of people who might not be familiar with the Hillsdale brand so they drafted a second copy treatment to explain why they teach the course, and how it is free. Then they launched an A/B test to see which copy treatment performed better.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that specificity does increase the force of the value proposition, which in turn increased donor conversion rate. This may not be the case with all of Hillsdale’s courses, but when attracting a new audience, we may need to be more educational in why the courses are presented, and how they are funded.

experiments.title: How adding credibility and clarity to the landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page produces the the greatest conversion rate to customers for their conferences?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2014 - 03/27/2014
The Acquire The Fire site is a WordPress multi-page installation that gave the user plenty of options: get resources, find out more about the ministry, see the latest list of tour dates, and access to the store (which sold curriculum, merchandise, and other non-ticket items). Sometimes, access to all of multiple pages is a good thing — it encourages users to spend more time on the site and learn more about the ministry. However, we hypothesized that this could lead to what we call “unsupervised thinking” — by allowing users to wander all over the site, we give them more opportunities to get distracted from the ultimate goal. We needed to be able to guide them down the “path of righteousness” that leads to conversion.
We wanted to create an experiment that helps determine what motivates their customers in order to increase their overall conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After just under a month of testing, this landing page delivered a 35% lift in visitor conversion! Additionally, the name acquisition offers grew Teen Mania’s email list size by 13%. This page was a resounding success, and taught us several things about Teen Mania customers:
1. Credibility matters. There are a lot of youth evangelism events out there, but few of them have the endorsements, coverage, and credibility that Acquire The Fire has. This needs to be up front and repeated throughout the value proposition.
2. Buying tickets is a big decision. Our customers are generally buying tickets for a large group and want to make a good decision with their budget dollars. The more that we can show them what they will experience and reinforce that with quotes and testimonials from people who have experienced an Acquire The Fire event, the more we can reduce anxiety about the event itself.
3. Not all visitors are ready to buy. We know anecdotally that it can take time for youth pastors to get budget approval, parental approval, or youth signups. Because of these delays, not everyone who comes to this site is able to buy tickets. We need ways to engage these visitors and give them something of value in exchange for their email address (and the opportunity to market to them later).

experiments.title: How the power of match language affects conversion

rows.name: "Double My Impact" Call to Action Copy
Action Oriented Call to Action Copy

experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesized that because of the urgent message and the clear impact of the campaign, more donors would respond to a call-to-action that showed the impact of their money.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2013 - 06/06/2013
Americans For Prosperity ran a one-day fundraising campaign that had a matching gift it — a generous donor had offered to double every gift. These are common in fundraising, and are usually a boost to any campaign — but we wondered how much the match actually incentivized the gift. Would more people give if they knew their gift was being matched, or would more people give to support the outcome they wanted?
We devised a test to see which treatment would convert more visitors to donors: match language or impact language?
experiments.keyLearnings: The new call-to-action decreased donor conversion 26.6%, showing that “doubling the gift” is a powerful motivational incentive. This led to a focused effort to secure more matching gifts for online campaigns and continuously promoting the match throughout the fundraising initiative.

experiments.title: How reduced email-specific landing page copy impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing the length of the value proposition on a email-specific landing page increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2017 - 08/16/2017
Hillsdale College discovered that creating channel-specific landing pages allowed them to launch tests and document test results with even greater accuracy. By splitting up the incoming traffic based on channel, they hoped they could match the experience closer to the motivation of the user. They hypothesized that individuals who received and read through an email offer about their online course, “Theology 101” may have a high enough motivation that they didn’t need to read a long value proposition on the landing page they clicked to visit. They launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale found that the treatment with the reduced value proposition actually decreased email acquisition by 26.2%. This would indicate that the highly motivated user still needs a little nudging even after clicking through the email offer.
The results of this test almost reached validity. However, since they did not officially reach the 95% confidence threshold, we cannot say with certainty that this reduction in negatively impacted conversion.
Despite falling short of validity, this test does provide a helpful learning. It’s clear that the reduction in copy was nearing significance. For that reason, we have launched additional tests similar to this one on a variety of channel-specific landing pages to track how copy length impacts email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How leveraging the power of the unknown affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does it improve conversion to suggest that the visitor has “never heard of” Imprimis?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2015 - 02/17/2015
Hillsdale College launched a Facebook campaign to grow the subscriber list for Imprimis, their renowned speech digest newsletter. Salon Magazine called Imprimis “the most influential conservative publication you’ve never heard of”, which Hillsdale wanted to use in the copy. Within that quote, there are two claims of value: that Imprimis is “the most influential conservative publication” and that the user has “never heard of” it. We decided to test the first claim independently and then measure the impact of adding the second claim to it.
experiments.keyLearnings: Leveraging the power of the unknown had a significant impact on landing page conversion. Overall conversion rate still leaves something to be desired (to be improved through further testing), but we know that adding an element of perceived mystery to the value proposition increases conversion.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition through a radical redesign affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which page layout and value proposition best presents the value proposition and minimizes friction and anxiety to optimize donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2015 - 02/23/2015
Hillsdale College’s popular Constitution 101 online course is supported by donations from individuals, many of whom donate after taking the course. Immediately after signing up for the course (or any of their other courses), registrants are given the opportunity to donate.  The original donation page contained multiple links to other parts of the site, concentrated in two different navigational areas. It also gave them an immediate opt-out opportunity — by clicking the link (in the second paragraph of copy) “I cannot make a donation at this time”.
A single line of copy asked the user to support the course, making the assumption that the visitor already understood the value of Hillsdale College. Additionally, the page focused on a premium book, the Constitution Reader, and used most of the available language to sell the value proposition of that item.
In order to improve donor conversion on this page, Hillsdale wanted to test a simplified design that expanded and highlighted the value proposition of Hillsdale College rather than the premium associated with their gift.
The new treatment page took away all of the navigation menus — creating a “cul-de-sac”, where the visitor either continues through the page or closes the window. The value proposition language focuses on the goal of the course and the intended outcome — educating 50 million Americans on Constitutional founding principles. The Constitution Reader offer is reduced to a secondary, value-added ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two donation forms resulted in nearly identical revenue -- but the new treatment led to a 33.3% increase in conversion rate.
Since the primary goal of the page is the acquisition of new donors, the increased conversion rate made the new treatment the winner. The discrepancy in average gifts between the pages did lead to some interesting observations.  After further examination of the individual transactions, we learned that the new treatment was getting lower average gifts than the control. This led us to believe that the three options in the new treatment's gift array may be too much of a stretch for most people.  As a result, they are using the "Other amount" option.  This is an example of "unsupervised thinking" which is leading to the visitors entering lower gift amounts than we would prefer.
As a result, Hillsdale's next experiment will be a test of the gift array to see if lowering the initial ask can lead to an overall increased average gift.

experiments.title: How a shortcut to the acquisition form impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a shortcut to the bottom of the email acquisition page increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2017 - 10/26/2017
The conversion rate for Econ 101 had historically remained around 25% for visitors to the page. This indicated that the visitors had a high motivation. We had the hypothesis that if we could make it easier to convert, we could acquire more email addresses. On mobile especially, the length of the page was a point of friction. We decided to add in navigation bar that would allow the visitor to jump to the bottom of the page to see if eliminating friction this way would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this situation, the addition of a “smooth scrolling” option that jumped visitors to the bottom of the page, decreased email acquisition by 8.3%. While this did not reach a level of statistical significance, it did not seem like it would be a positive impact in either case so we decided to move on to the next experiment. There are multiple reasons why this treatment may not have worked:
The jump to the bottom skipped past the value proposition copy which decreased their likelihood to convert.
The length of the page was not a significant point of friction to start with which meant this change did not fix an actual existing problem.
The thick underline created a visual divider between the headline and copy that interrupted the visual flow down the page.
Visitors did not understand that the arrow was an actual shortcut on the page.
Regardless of the reason, this test would indicate that the “shortcut” to the bottom does not increase the chances of conversion.

experiments.title: How a related background image impacts email acquisition rate on a course signup page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the presence of a related background image increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2017 - 10/26/2017
Hillsdale College was looking for new ways to optimize their online course signup page for ‘Great Books 102.’ They wondered: Will the presence of a related background image increase email acquisition rate? They launched a treatment page that contained the same image used in their Facebook promotion and tracked the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale College found that the presence of a related background image did not increase email acquisition rate on their ‘Great Books 102’ course signup page. Even though the imagery selected for the background does match the image used in the Facebook advertisement, it’s clear that visitors were not significantly affected by the addition of the background image. Therefore, it did not reduce anxiety like it was intended to do.
Further tests are being conducted to determine if any kind of background image is effective in reducing anxiety.

experiments.title: How message tone of an email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does messaging tone affect donation response rate in an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2015 - 01/22/2015
As  part of their new monthly email appeal strategy, Hillsdale College wanted to learn which messaging tone produced the best response rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment utilized two distinct emails and two distinct landing pages. In the experiment, the softer tone email generated 257% increase in click through rate.  This increase in traffic illustrates a concept that we have observed in other experiments.  By reducing the "sharpness" of the ask in the email, we can increase the volume of traffic to the landing page, however the visitors will be less motivated causing the on-page conversion rate to be lower than the Control.   However, if the increase in traffic is significant enough, the net result will be more revenue.
The softer tone treatment delivered a response rate 85% higher than the original control with a 40% higher average gift.
This experiment is a great example of the importance of analyzing the entire conversion pathway, from email delivery to successful gift, when determining a winner.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of the value proposition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which of the three value propositions, backed by evidentials, will increase both average gift and conversion rate the greatest amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2014 - 07/20/2014
Despite many experiments, the control version of the main Heritage Foundation donation form had consistently maintained the highest conversion rate. In an effort to influence both conversion rate and average gift, we developed and tested multiple value propositions and backed them with evidentials.
experiments.keyLearnings: The research aspect of Heritage's efforts had the biggest impact on conversion rate. The main donation form will use this language moving forward and this aspect of Heritage's mission will be emphasized in future campaigns when communicating with donors.

experiments.title: How hard versus. soft calls to action affect click rates in prospecting emails

rows.name: Activate your free course now CTA

experiments.researchQuestion: Does a hard or a soft call to action drive higher click through rates in a prospecting email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2016 - 08/25/2016
Hillsdale College has had great results with prospecting to third party email lists for their Online Course offerings.  Building on recent learnings from Facebook advertising, we decided to test whether a soft or hard call to action would deliver a higher click rate.
The soft version used “Learn More” as the call to action.  The treatment version used a more direct “Activate your free course now” call to action. In Facebook testing the “Learn More” version had driven higher click rates. The hypothesis on that test was that people aren’t fully comfortable committing to an action from a Facebook ad, but would be willing to take a low-commitment action to learn more.
We wanted to see if this same hypothesis would hold true on a prospecting email when the reader has more information before deciding whether to click through. This test was carried out by an A/B split mailing with one of our prospecting partners.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As expected, the softer CTA “Learn more” drove slightly higher click rates. However, an unintended result of the test is that the Learn More version got a 27% lower conversion rate on the landing page.
We were originally planning to focus our learnings on CTR. But since there was not a significant difference (3.2% difference) we decided to focus on landing page conversion rate because that is where we saw the biggest distinction in results.
By clarifying the call to action in the email, recipients had about the same likelihood of taking the next step of clicking.  But when they arrived at the landing page they were much more motivated to complete the final action of enrolling in the course.

experiments.title: How a campaign progress meter affects the average donation amount
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the use of a progress meter impact overall giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2015 - 07/01/2015
During their fiscal year end campaign, Hillsdale College had set a target revenue total as a way of establishing the need. Historically, we had used a progress meter found at the top of the page as a way of communicating both the goal and the progress toward it. As the end of the campaign approached, we wanted to determine if these progress meters had the impact on revenue that we assumed they did. Knowing this would help us with future campaigns.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test was not able to reach a statistically valid level before the end of the campaign but, it was quickly approaching validity. With this in mind, we would hypothesize that the progress meter did have an impact on the overall revenue of the campaign. There did not seem to be much of an impact to donor conversion but those that did give, gave at a higher amount.

experiments.title: How a testing into a new landing page affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation landing page will produce the greatest amount of donors and dollars?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2015 - 06/08/2015
Hillsdale College had recently launched a new microsite to handle all of their online giving. The new microsite had a variation of their standard design but made it easier to create contextualized donation forms and took into account certain principles we learned from previous experiments such as removing banners and navigation, adding value proposition language, and an easier to use donation form.
One of their more traffic donation forms was a general donation form for their online classes.  Each class has its own donation form but, to handle general website traffic, we wanted one that could be applicable to all courses.  Before making the switch to the new microsite, we wanted to validate that the new page would achieve the lift in conversion rate and revenue that we were expecting.
experiments.keyLearnings: The learnings achieved through previous experiments held true with this new landing page and help achieve a 35.8% increase in donation conversion rate. The experiment was a good example of leveraging the learnings from multiple experiments to craft a new, more effective landing page.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition on a donation page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition is most appealing to donors to increase the donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2015 - 06/05/2015
For Hillsdale College, the Constitution 101 online course has proven to be a great source of new email addresses and new donors for their file.  In particular, the landing page that is shown after someone signs up has the highest volume of traffic compared to their other donation offers. As a result, this page has been the focus of multiple testing iterations over the past several months.
The first big lift to conversion rate came from modifying the value proposition displayed on the original page. After that initial experiment, we tried experimenting with different tactical elements of the page. None of these experiments were able to boost results.
Since the value proposition was proven to be the best way to boost results so we wanted to try refining this value proposition even further. We created a new copy treatment that removed the organizational goals and instead explored the reasoning for the course in greater depth.
experiments.keyLearnings: By removing the organizational goal of having 50 million people take the class and instead emphasizing why it is so critical that the class be offered for free, we were able to boost conversion rate by 22.6%. This is a good example of examining the message through the eyes of the donor to focus in on what is most important to them.  The “50 million people” goal wasn’t necessarily bad but this test proves it was the deeper value of the course that they valued more.

experiments.title: How a radically different message approach affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radically different message approach on the main donation page improve the conversion rate and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2015 - 04/17/2015
The Hillsdale College donation page made the assumption than once a visitor made it to the page, they were committed to making their gift.  As a result, the copy on the page mentioned other ways they could make a gift but did not try to reinforce the visitors decision to make that gift.
In an effort to improve overall performance of the page, we decided to run an experiment testing out multiple elements of the page.  The goal was to reduce the friction that came with the out-of-the-box donation form (making it mobile-friendly and easier to use), remove choices the visitor had to make by removing the navigation, and improve the value proposition expressed on the page by talking about what was appealing and exclusive to Hillsdale.
experiments.keyLearnings: The biggests lifts will often come when trying a totally different approach.  This radical redesign tested multiple factors in order to improve the overall conversion experience which resulted in a 22% lift to conversion and an 18.9% lift to average gift.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition through a radical redesign affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which page layout and value proposition best presents the value proposition and minimizes friction and anxiety to optimize donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2015 - 02/23/2015
Hillsdale College’s popular Constitution 101 online course is supported by donations from individuals, many of whom donate after taking the course. Immediately after signing up for the course (or any of their other courses), registrants are given the opportunity to donate.  The original donation page contained multiple links to other parts of the site, concentrated in two different navigational areas. It also gave them an immediate opt-out opportunity — by clicking the link (in the second paragraph of copy) “I cannot make a donation at this time”. A single line of copy asked the user to support the course, making the assumption that the visitor already understood the value of Hillsdale College. Additionally, the page focused on a premium book, the Constitution Reader, and used most of the available language to sell the value proposition of that item.
In order to improve donor conversion on this page, Hillsdale wanted to test a simplified design that expanded and highlighted the value proposition of Hillsdale College rather than the premium associated with their gift.
The new treatment page took away all of the navigation menus — creating a “cul-de-sac”, where the visitor either continues through the page or closes the window. The value proposition language focuses on the goal of the course and the intended outcome — educating 50 million Americans on Constitutional founding principles. The Constitution Reader offer is reduced to a secondary, value-added ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two donation forms resulted in nearly identical revenue — but the new treatment led to a 33.3% increase in conversion rate. Since the primary goal of the page is the acquisition of new donors, the increased conversion rate made the new treatment the winner.
The discrepancy in average gifts between the pages did lead to some interesting observations.  After further examination of the individual transactions, we learned that the new treatment was getting lower average gifts than the control. This led us to believe that the three options in the new treatment’s gift array may be too much of a stretch for most people.  As a result, they are using the “Other amount” option.  This is an example of “unsupervised thinking” which is leading to the visitors entering lower gift amounts than we would prefer.
As a result, Hillsdale’s next experiment will be a test of the gift array to see if lowering the initial ask can lead to an overall increased average gift.

experiments.title: How leveraging the power of the unknown affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does it improve conversion to suggest that the visitor has “never heard of” Imprimis?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2015 - 02/17/2015
Hillsdale College launched a Facebook campaign to grow the subscriber list for Imprimis, their renowned speech digest newsletter. Salon Magazine called Imprimis “the most influential conservative publication you’ve never heard of”, which Hillsdale wanted to use in the copy. Within that quote, there are two claims of value: that Imprimis is “the most influential conservative publication” and that the user has “never heard of” it. We decided to test the first claim independently and then measure the impact of adding the second claim to it.
experiments.keyLearnings: Leveraging the power of the unknown had a significant impact on landing page conversion. Overall conversion rate still leaves something to be desired (to be improved through further testing), but we know that adding an element of perceived mystery to the value proposition increases conversion.

experiments.title: How message tone of an email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does messaging tone affect donation response rate in an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2015 - 01/22/2015
As  part of their new monthly email appeal strategy, Hillsdale College wanted to learn which messaging tone produced the best response rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment utilized two distinct emails and two distinct landing pages. In the experiment, the softer tone email generated 257% increase in click through rate. This increase in traffic illustrates a concept that we have observed in other experiments.  By reducing the “sharpness” of the ask in the email, we can increase the volume of traffic to the landing page, however the visitors will be less motivated causing the on-page conversion rate to be lower than the Control.   However, if the increae in traffic is significant enough, the net result will be more revenue.
The softer tone treatment delivered a response rate 85% higher than the original control with a 40% higher average gift. This experiment is a great example of the importance of analyzing the entire conversion pathway, from email delivery to successful gift, when determining a winner.

experiments.title: How reducing form friction on email acquisition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does dividing the registration form in to multiple steps increase the overall acquisition of emails, addresses, and donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/08/2015 - 07/07/2015
Hillsdale College’s Constitution 101 course is one of the biggest source of new emails and new donors for the college. Previous experiments had been able to increase the conversion rate of the donation page and had increased the number of visitors to the page.  However, we had been unable to build upon the success of the initial treatment created for the name acquisition page.
We decided to create another treatment that would divide the existing form into two steps; name and email would be first, followed by the participant’s address. When we devised this experiment, we made sure to set up tracking across every step of the process to ensure any improvements in one area didn’t adversely impact the other important metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two step form successfully increased every metric along the registration process.  Email acquisition increased by 17.6% and the number of home addresses acquired increased by 9.0%. The biggest lift was achieved with the final step of the process with a 93.4% increase in donor conversion.

 

By spreading out the steps, we reduced the amount of mental friction associated with each step of the process. The forms looked shorter and, as such, increased the ultimate conversion along each step.

experiments.title: How a broader value proposition in the headline affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a broader value statement in the headline increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2016 - 05/03/2016
Hillsdale College was promoting their free Constitution 101 course through multiple online channels. The control signup page converted nearly 40% of visitors to enroll in the course, but it also assumed that the primary value proposition, “Understand the Constitution like never before” resonated with each visitor.
They wanted to test whether a broader statement might capture the attention of more people who did not already have an inclination to “understand the Constitution like never before” and convince more to enroll. They also hypothesized that a “value statement” in the headline might result in more donations. So they created a second page, with the headline “The Constitution secures your freedoms as an American” and the subheadline “Understand it like never before in Hillsdale College’s free online course”.
They launched an A/B test to determine the winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new page with the broader headline actually decreased email acquisition conversion rate by 2.7%. However, looking at the entire funnel, we noticed that visitors to this page were 14.4% more likely to donate.
This presented an interesting finding: while the broader statement resulted in fewer course enrollments, it increased the motivation of those who did enroll. This seems to suggest that while the new headline was more divisive among initial visitors (which caused slightly lower enrollment), it increased the appeal of the course by playing to the values (rather than just the surface desires) of those who accepted the offer. This reiterates the importance of measuring the entire funnel when there is both email acquisition and donor acquisition at stake.

experiments.title: Validating a series of experiment results using an A/A experiment
experiments.researchQuestion: Is there an instrumentation error that has prevented the new ATA template from properly validating an experiment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/14/2015 - 08/19/2015
After a series of experiments to optimize the Imprimis survey, we started to believe the new ATA survey template may be causing errors. We decided to run a straight A/A test where both the control and treatment were identical. If we still managed to create a statistically significant lift, we would know the template was causing errors.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment produced two statistically valid lifts for both donations and number of survey completions. This result leads us to the conclusion that the new template must be causing issues with the way the A/B testing platform is collecting data. Most likely, this is the result of how the survey was added to the page in an iFrame.  The results of all previous experiments should be ignored.

experiments.title: Does moving the form further down increase sign ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does moving the form further down the enrollment page allow the eye to more easily see the course descriptions, which may motivate them to enroll?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/20/2018 - 04/19/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to see if changing the layout on the sign up page for their Constitutional Conversations course would impact conversions. They decided to test endorsements behind a white background versus a blue background, and made the other course description boxes larger on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The mobile versions of these layouts do not include the quotes or course descriptions, so both versions look identical. Even with a larger sample size, this experiment can’t be validated. In the future, any layout tests should exclude the any traffic that wouldn’t be impacted by the test. In the future, a new test to consider would be a treatment without course descriptions to see if they impact enrollment.

experiments.title: How a “monthly-only” form affects recurring gift conversion during a recurring gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a “monthly-only” form affect conversion during a recurring gift campaign?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
Hillsdale College was running a campaign to increase membership to their recurring gift club, the Liberty and Learning Society. Their call center had noted that many people in the past who made recurring gifts did so believing that they were one-time gifts, and called to cancel. Therefore, they suggested that the form be a “tabbed” donation form that they defaulted to a monthly gift, but still allowed one-time gifts. The team wanted to test this against a “monthly-only” form to see if the lack of a one-time option moved more people towards one-time gifts. Or, put another way, they wanted to see if having a one-time option acted as a bailout mechanism that gave people an “out” to give a short-term gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the monthly-only option, had no effect on the amount of recurring gifts submitted through the form. This indicated that, at least in the near term, there was no confusion about usage of the tabbed form. As an additional bonus, many people who did not make recurring gifts did make one-time gifts as well, leading to another source of revenue for the campaign, though it was not aligned with the intent of the campaign.
The caveat to this test comes in that it will take some time to see if the tabbed form generated as many downstream donor cancellation calls as the monthly form, though that data can be tied back to the original source form.

experiments.title: Mug Signer Test Revenue (Low-Dollar Donor)
How increasing “involving” score affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing “involving” score affect email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/14/2020
Hillsdale College was running a pre-registration campaign for their new course, “Introduction to Western Philosophy”. They had run a test that determined that a “problem-solution” framework increased clickthrough rate, and thus signups and donors. However, they had been informed of a partner who had a method of testing copy on a 2×2 framework that indicated that increasing “narrative” score and “involving” score would increase fundraising conversion. The partner put together a treatment that, through their scoring mechanism, significantly increased “involving” score. They decided to test this copy to see if it had a positive effect on name acquisition, which would get more names into the donor conversion funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the higher “involving” score produced a slight, but not statistically significant decrease in email acquisition rate. When the team looked at the full funnel, there was a slight, but not statistically valid increase in donor conversion later in the process.
Ultimately, there wasn’t a significant difference at either step of the funnel. This may have been because of one of two factors:
The two-step soft offer of preregistration followed by ask might have muted the copy’s effect on donor conversion by placing the involving copy too far from the ask.
The copy that produced the higher involving score also lowered the narrative score, which may have had an adverse effect on conversion.
This shows a path to testing future direct ask copy with this method, provided that both involving and narrative scores both increase.

experiments.title: How including an image influences conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including a related image on the course registration page improve email acquisition rate for the Jane Austen online course?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2019 - 09/17/2019
For Hillsdale’s free course offer, we wanted to test increasing clarity through providing an image related to the course subject that was used to promote the offer on the email acquisition page. The control version used the same copy and contained no image. Our hypothesis was that adding clarity and familiarizing the audience with the author of the works covered in the course content would increase the perceived value of the offer. We wanted to see if showing a black and white illustrated image would act as a distraction and introduce friction or improve conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a month, we were not able to validate the results of the experiment and will need to pursue further testing with this hypothesis.
Adding some clarity to the offer—enough to remind the visitors of the topic that would be covered in the course and increase the perceived value of the offer—without acting as a distraction, did result in additional course registrations on the treatment page,

experiments.title: How intellectually-focused copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will intellectually-focused copy affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/20/2019 - 06/29/2019
Hillsdale College had developed copy for their fiscal year end campaign, designed to be donor-centric. However, as the copy went through their editing process, it was changed to include more intellectual language that elevated the reading level and put the College at the center, instead of the donor. Hillsdale wanted to test the “pre-edit” copy against the “post-edit” copy, and created two landing pages to split traffic to throughout the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The intellectually-focused copy produced a valid decrease of 12% early in the campaign, but later “high urgency” emails diluted both the impact and the level of confidence by increasing motivation. This resulted in a directional learning, but one that’s important to consider for future copy.

experiments.title: How adding an image of an offer in an email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding an image of an offer in an email affect click rate to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2019
Hillsdale College recently launched a new online course titled “The Second World Wars.” They are now offering this course as a DVD boxed set and are offering it as a premium offer to their housefile. They wanted to know whether or not adding an image of the DVD boxed set to the email would have any effect on click rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a 4% lift in the treatment version with the image of the DVD. The confidence level of this experiment was not at or above the 98th percentile, so this experiment shows no change in results. Even with a larger sample size, this test is not likely to validate. This is because there is such a small difference in click rate that a larger sample size will not determine any valid conclusion. This image test can be run instead on the landing page where people actually convert. We hypothesize that people like to see visuals with text as it helps them better understand what is being asked of them/offered to them.

experiments.title: How linking one line of text instead of two affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will having only one linked line of text in email copy affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/26/2019 - 07/10/2019
Hillsdale College ran a fiscal year end campaign. In this campaign, they versioned emails to lifecycle audiences: lapsed donors, active donors, and nondonors. In their email to active donors, they wanted to know if linking one line of text to a donation page instead of two or more lines would affect click rate. They decided to test this on their third email appeal in the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a 5.8% increase in clickthrough rate in the treatment with only one linked line of text to a donation page. However, this is not a large difference and the level of confidence is insufficient to conclude this experiment.

experiments.title: The impact of direct mail cultivation on overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Does sending cultivation and stewardship-focused post cards increase revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2018 - 06/11/2019
For the complete writeup, visit https://app.winstonknows.com/e/z7Nd.
Hillsdale College was looking for new ways to help retain existing donors. Knowing the importance of good cultivation and stewardship, we wanted to focus on methods that could improve the frequency and method for communicating impact and gratitude to the donors. One hypothesis was to send post cards throughout the year that both thanked them for their support and reminded them of the impact their gift.
To do this, we isolated 350K donors and split it randomly in such a way that there would be equal representation for each donor lifecycle segment. Half of these donors would receive four post cards throughout the year in addition to the normal communication stream. The control group would just get the standard communications.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant change to overall revenue. We did observe a 1.8% increase to donor retention / reactivation but the decrease in revenue per donor resulted in minimal difference between overall revenue.

experiments.title: How a text endorsement compares to a video endorsement for conversion impact
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a text endorsement and/or video endorsement impact sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2018 - 06/02/2018
This experiment was tested on the “Introduction to the Constitution” online course sign up page. We had found in previous experiments that videos negatively impacted a prospects likelihood to make a gift but we had seen some indication that they could help improve email acquisition if used in the right way. We had a video that provided an endorsement for the new online course and we wanted to see if it could help boost email acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings: Overall, there was not enough difference in conversions to suggest that the video treatment made any impact. After running for a full month, we decided to move on to the next hypothesis since it did not see to make any significant improvements.

experiments.title: How Faith-Driven Copy Affects Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a donation page more focused on the religious value proposition of “C.S. Lewis: Writings and Significance” increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/23/2017 - 08/31/2017
Due to lower traffic volumes, this test did not validate. Further testing will be necessary to provide an answer to this research question. However, Hillsdale can still consider the results that they found. Though there wasn’t a statistically significant difference, Hillsdale did notice that the faith-focused copy was approaching a valid decrease in donor conversion.
This decrease in donor conversion may suggest that the treatment page copy did not increase the clarity of the course’s value proposition. It may also indicate that visitors felt an increase in tension if they did not align with the overtly religious value proposition. New tests are being developed to provide a clearer answer.
experiments.keyLearnings: After noticing new room for improvement, Hillsdale College launched a series of new donation page treatments. “C.S. Lewis: Writings and Significance” is one of their most popular online courses. After reviewing their donation page, they hypothesized that emphasizing why they offer this course from a religious viewpoint would help clarify the course’s value proposition for visitors. They created a treatment and launched a test to find out.

experiments.title: How matching gift language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will added matching grant language affect donor conversion after an online course signup?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 11/23/2017
Hillsdale College had received a matching grant of $400,000 to double gifts from their online courses. They wondered what the impact of adding this language to their online course donation pages would be—their hypothesis is that it would increase giving by giving donors an incentive to be generous.
They created treatments across three course donation pages that included the extra language for the matching grant and launched an experiment to determine the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The matching gift language produced a slight, though statistically invalid decrease in conversion. A meta-analysis of Hillsdale’s online course pages showed that it was critical to not “break the spell” of excitement after signing up for a course.
Though matching gift language might work in other areas of fundraising, it might cause the prospect to think too much about the donation, thus breaking the spell and reducing conversion.

experiments.title: How suggesting impacting in the giving array impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Does aligning the gift array to specific impact increase overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2016 - 04/08/2016
For the Hillsdale moneybomb dedicated to raising funds for the DRTV campaign, we had a unique opportunity for the college. We were able to assign impact to the amount of money given; for $70, a donor could help secure a single DRTV spot.
Since we are not often able to assign specific impact to a gift, we decided to test what aligning the gift array to this impact would have on revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test had a limited amount of time that we could run so we were not able to reach statistical validity. What we observed was that the gift handles that tied the gift to the DRTV spot received less revenue per visitor.
After examining the actual gifts given, we found that we lost smaller gift donors with the treatment. This would be the result of the gift array starting at $70 instead of $25 which is our control. This would not normally be an issue if we can significantly raise the average gift but, unfortunately, this treatment did not do that.

experiments.title: Student vs. Celebrity 3rd Party Credibility
experiments.researchQuestion: Which 3rd party credibility source has the most influence on email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/18/2016 - 11/18/2016
In the ongoing effort to improve the Constitution 101 email acquisition efforts, we wanted to see which source would be best to lend 3rd party credibility. To do this, we tested the standard Rush Limbaugh quote against quotes from students that reinforced the value of the course.
experiments.keyLearnings: The student quotes showed no significant difference over the control. Nothing to change here.

experiments.title: How adding gift handles to a landing page affected conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a suggested giving amount increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2019 - 03/02/2019
Hillsdale College recently mailed out thousands of letters to school administrators and teachers across America. In these letters, they offered to send a free pocket-sized copy of the U.S. Constitution for every single child in every classroom. Hillsdale received an astonishing number of replies requesting copies of the Constitution. To fund the shipment to all of these schools, Hillsdale launched a four-day email campaign asking donors to send a donation to help send pocket-sized Constitutions to schoolchildren across the U.S. They wanted to know if donations would increase on the landing page with the addition of a brief explanation of how many pocket Constitutions could be sent to children with a certain donation amount. They thought that if they suggested a giving amount next to how many pocket Constitutions could be sent with that amount, it would help the donor make a decision that they would otherwise have to make on their own. They hypothesized that telling people the impact a gift amount would make would inspire people to give generously, thus increasing donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Hillsdale team saw a 7.2% drop in conversion with the gift handle treatment. This was after sending all traffic from the second email of the campaign to the treatment landing page, which suggests that there would be an even larger decrease in conversion had the traffic been split evenly in that second email. After the test had run for three days, the Hillsdale team decided to end the test in favor of launching a new test against the control: the addition of a photo in the landing page. This new experiment can be found here.

experiments.title: How adding location to a seminar sign up affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the word “Constitution” and the location of the seminar in the sign up box increase sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2018 - 08/22/2018
Hillsdale College was promoting a Lifelong Learning Seminar. They wanted to increase sign ups, so they created an experiment to test adding additional information about the seminar — the descriptor “Constitution”, and the location of the seminar. They wondered if adding more information would make people more likely to sign up.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was insufficient traffic to the pages to validate this experiment.

experiments.title: How adding context to a video endorsement impacts sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding copy under a video endorsement impact sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2018 - 06/27/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to know if adding copy to an endorsement video on the “Introduction to the Constitution” sign up page would increase the number of sign ups. They decided to test one sign up page with copy versus another sign up page with no copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a large enough sample size to validate this experiment. However, with a conversion rate of 30.2% with the copy version and a 33.1% conversation rate with the version without copy, it’s likely that with more traffic, this experiment could validate in favor of no copy with video endorsements in the future. The test would need to run longer in order to prove this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How different endorsement methods impact sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a text endorsement and/or video endorsement impact sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2018 - 05/08/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to know if adding an endorsement on the “Introduction to the Constitution” sign up page would increase the number of sign ups. They decided to test different endorsement styles, quotes vs videos vs a combination of both.
experiments.keyLearnings: Overall, there was not enough difference in conversions to suggest that the endorsement treatments made any impact. However, there was not a large enough sample size to validate this experiment. With more data, this experiment could likely validate in the future.

experiments.title: How extra formatting in the email affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will extra formatting in the email affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2018 - 04/25/2018
This experiment was tested in the email copy. Hillsdale College wanted to see if adding bold text and two extra links in the copy would increase click rates. We hyperlinked two phrases in the original copy and added some boldness to the original text to see what impact would be made.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment had a .47% click rate in comparison to a .44% click rate. We can conclude that there is no difference in the bolded version and the original version with no bold lettering. After a side by side comparison, the additional formatting was not remarkably different. As a result, it provided minimal impact on the readers likelihood to take action.

experiments.title: How a change in layout plus addition of video affects sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the layout and adding video to a page affect sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2018 - 04/20/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to increase conversions on their “Constitutional Conversations” online course sign up page. They wanted to know if removing endorsements and other offers from the enrollment page would impact conversions. They tested the control, which included endorsements and other offers, against a treatment with only the sign up form, a video, and short copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant difference between the control and treatment versions in this experiment. What’s more, since there were multiple factors being tested, it is hard to know if one of the aspects (video vs. no video and endorsement vs. no endorsement) would have moved the needle by itself. In the future, we will want to isolate those individual variables as separate tests to understand their specific impact.

experiments.title: How adding an endorsement increases sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an endorsement increase sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2018 - 05/04/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to see if an endorsement on the sign up page would affect sign ups for the Founding Documents offer. This treatment was tested with the following signers: Laura Ingraham, Dennis Prager, Rich Bott, Rush Limbaugh, and Eric Metaxas. The individual endorsements were shown based upon the source of the traffic so that visitors that were directed by Laura Ingraham would see her endorsement.
experiments.keyLearnings: When analyzing the results across all pages, we found that removing the endorsement from the page resulted in no significant impact to email acquisition. All versions that removed a signer endorsement decreased except for Dennis Prager’s, which saw a 4% lift in sign ups without the endorsement (not statistically significant. This is an important reminder to always validate “best practices.” We had always looked for authoritative endorsement but, in this situation, it would appear not to be a factor in the conversion process.

experiments.title: How removing context of a video affects sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding information about video content impact sign ups? Are people motivated by curiosity, or do they like to know what they’re getting into?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2018 - 06/27/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to get more sign ups for their online course “Introduction to C.S. Lewis.” They wanted to know if providing information about the video on the page, rather than no context at all, would impact sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings: Insufficient data to produce takeaway. Need to push more traffic to validate.

experiments.title: How adding more information in the sign up section increases sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding information about the seminar location increase sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2018 - 08/01/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to see if giving more information in the sign up box — the location of the seminar — would lead to more sign ups. They added a treatment to see what would happen.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although there was not enough traffic to validate this experiment, the current conversion rates of 5.6% for the control and 4.8% for the treatment show that there was no statistically significant difference between adding the location of the seminar in the sign up box.

experiments.title: How problem-centric language affects donor conversion at year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: How will problem-centric language affects donor conversion at year-end?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2018 - 12/30/2018
Hillsdale College was trying to increase conversion for their donation pages during their year-end campaign. They had previously seen that problem-centric language, or explaining the challenge they were trying to solve, had increased conversion in a fiscal-year-end campaign. However, they were unsure how that would affect results at a very different time of year, so they decided to test it.
They created a page that started with the problem, rather than positioning Hillsdale as the solution and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The problem-centric language produced an 8.4% decrease in conversion, though it didn’t reach statistical validity. However, due to the time-sensitive nature of the year-end ask, they decided to continue using the control as not to lose any revenue during peak times.
Previous testing has shown that problem-centric language can increase conversion, but a calendar year-end campaign might not be the best time to try it.

experiments.title: How a subject line test of a famous author’s first novel vs the root of their creative genius affected opens

rows.name: The roots of Jane Austen's genius

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a subject line test of a famous author’s first novel vs the root of their creative genius increase opens?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2018 - 04/20/2018
This experiment was conducted to see if a change in subject line between Jane Austen’s first novel and the root of her creative genius had any effect on open rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: The open rate for the control was 14% and the open rate for the treatment was 14.1%. This means there was no significant difference in responses to the subject lines. For future experiments, we will need to test more radical changes to the subject line.

experiments.title: How hyperlink text color affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: In this experiment, we asked how the color of a hyperlink text in an email affects click-through rate? Our hypothesis was that changing the color from the traditional blue to red would grab the reader’s attention and increase incentive with a more urgent feeling.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2017 - 08/09/2017
A study we saw indicated that changing the hyperlink text color from the traditional blue to red increased the click-through rate of the email by 52%. So we decided to test this on one of our small contact list chase emails.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not validate due to a minimal lift and the small sample size of the test. This does not give us confidence to change all hyperlink text colors going forward.

experiments.title: How instrumentation error affects a donation button test
